description: Long filaments from behind dorsal and anal fins (shorter with age). Small diffuse dark spot on upper gill cover.
distinctive_features: Body shape: short and / or deep

description: Rounded bell, wider than it is tall. Purple radial canals. Short primary tentacles, long thin secondary tentacles.
Silvery body with prominent dark fins. Oblique dark bar from snout, through eye to dorsal fin. Mostly pelagic.
distinctive_features: Body shape: short and / or deep

description: Pale cone with short pointed spire. Pattern of small red to brownish spots or dashes covering the final whorl.
Brownish-gray parapodia barely cover a speckled shell. Buries in the sand and mud, mostly in shallow areas.
Extremely thin fan-shaped valves with rounded tops and faint radiating ribs. Translucent amber to gray in color.
Flat-bottomed conical shell, typically with other shells and pieces of coral debris cemented on for protection.
Pale translucent body. Straight rostrum, slightly curved at the tip. Stalked eyes. Distinctively elongated claw arms.
Distinctive red stripes from antennae and through eyes, joining on tail. Found on Corkscrew Anemones (p. 121).
The central disc has five distinctive lobes bulging out between the arms. Long spines on arms. Color variable.
Bushy colony of long tapering branches. Each branch divided into X or Y shape when seen in cross section.
Pink to dark red and may have a pale stripe. Claw arms sometimes banded with yellow. Distinctively long claws.
Pale reddish-orange with dark red stripes. Distinctive curved red line where abdomen slopes down to tail.
Translucent brown dorsum with white spots. Distinctive brown reticulated pattern, continuing onto rhinophores.
Slender orange body banded in red. Long thin legs. Often found on Giant Barrel Sponges (p. 64) at night.
Translucent dorsum with dark brown speckles. Mantle fringed with white and yellow, with larger black spots.
Pale oblong valves with tiny spines on radiating ribs. Long pink to reddish tentacles, often with faint white banding.
Flattened yellow to light brown valves with wavy streaks. Widely spaced low radial ribs. May attach to gorgonians.
Smooth tan to purplish dome, may have yellow spots. Spiny dark brown arms, spines are banded with yellow.
Mottled brown with three dark saddles. Row of three blotches on lower flank. Dash-like spots along body.
Transparent cerata with irregular white-tipped edges. Long oral tentacles. Feeds on Watercress Alga (p. 23).
White shell with scattered brown blotches. Evenly sized concentric ribs run to a slightly scalloped shell edge.
Green or yellowish body with conical white papillae and sometimes blue spots. Often on Caulerpa algae (p. 13).
Brown to purplish stalks with fine filamentous hair-like branchlets. Grows in clusters on shallower reefs.
Robust shell with a large body whorl and vertical ribs. Sawtooth aperture lip. Feeds on bivalves (seen above).
Smooth uniformly dark orange to reddish body. Broad flattened oral veil. Found in areas of sand and rubble.
Broken pinkish-white line down dorsum. Blue spots on edge of foot. Spots on long oral tentacles. From Florida.
Reddish body and yellow tail. Long pointed snout. Yellow stripe from snout to nape. Three black spots at rear dorsal fin.
Long slender body with a distinctive hump towards tail. Long tapering snout. Color varies, matching the host.

Maximum depth from Ref. 116012. Zooxanthellate (Ref. 116012). Occurs in lower reef slopes, soft substrates (Ref. 848) and hard substrate (Ref. 116012), occasionally on deep reefs and shaded walls (Ref. 415).
Smooth brown valves with bright green margins. An invasive species, recently introduced to the Caribbean.
Rigid pink to grayish stalks with densely packed short pointed branchlets. Gametophyte stage shown above.
Pale to reddish elongated body. Triangular fins towards a distinctively long pointed rear. Pelagic, rarely on reefs.
Dark purple to black body with some faint mottling. Can sometimes spread its parapodial wings to swim away.
Short arms and tentacles on a compact body, often with tiny black spots. Rounded fins connect at rear of body.
Dark body with large eye and prominent white spots along lateral line. Large oblong glowing light organ under eye.
Smooth tan shell with scattered dark spots. Light brown mantle. Black tentacles. Also found in solid gray to black.
Thick fan-shaped valves with large radial ribs. White with red or grayish lines. Found in shallow/intertidal areas.
Distinctively long narrow arms. Gray to light brown with widely spaced white spots. Usually found in sandy areas.
Zooxanthellate (Ref. 116012).

Iridescent reddish-brown body. Centipede-like legs on each body segment. Found in areas of shallow rubble. Bobbit worms make a den of strong fibers, appearing like spider’s silk. They are largely nocturnal hunters as most of their predators are fish that are active during the day.
Shell may be solid brown or with bands. Broad brown to black mantle with irregular white spots and long blotches.
A colony of thousands of tunicate clones, joined to form a long cylindrical structure. Pelagic, rarely seen over reefs.
Rounded silvery body with dark bars. Dark edges on long dorsal and anal fins. Often found in small dense schools.
distinctive_features: Body shape: short and / or deep

description: Rounded deep red valves with numerous pointed spines on both top and bottom. Brownish mantle with white blotches.
Silvery blue with thin dark stripes. Spot above gill cover. Black tips on tail. Thin trailing filament behind dorsal fin.
Pale brown body with dark blotches and lines. Brown bar through eye. Mimics floating sargassum or dead leaves.
distinctive_features: Body shape: short and / or deep

description: Large pointed spiral shell with patterns of wavy cream and reddish-brown bands. Mottled brown mantle.
Dark brown valves with a distinctive point near the hinge. Found attached to gorgonians, often heavily encrusted.
Yellow shell with pale blotches. Reddish mantle with numerous highly branched projections. Found on rubble.
Occurs chiefly on coral reefs starting at a depth of 18 m, often with bluish-pink iridescence (Ref. 85482).
Green to reddish-brown body with a pattern of pale conical knobs, each outlined in white. Prefers shallows.
Elongated glossy shell and pale mantle. Thin orange line runs along mantle edge. (Previously C. bahamensis.)
Tall first dorsal fin. White forehead. Two small ocelli on rear dorsal fin. Single stalk eye cirrus. No nape cirrus.
Olive to brown with small black spots. Brown bar through eye. Dark patches above pectoral and at dorsal base.
distinctive_features: Body shape: short and / or deep

description: Bluish upper body. Elongated lower jaw with a bright orange or red tip. Upper lobe of tail is orange or yellow.
Short first dorsal fin. Rear dorsal fin: one small ocellus or none. Eye and nape cirri are flattened bifurcated tabs.
Gelatinous bell with nematocyst warts. Four tentacles with red banding (may be retracted while swimming).
Silvery white with thin black chevron lines. Four black bars: through eye, behind pectoral, above anal and at tail base.
distinctive_features: Body shape: short and / or deep

description: Carapace and legs are hairy. Smooth red-banded claws. Most commonly found under larger anemones (p. 116).
Rear body and claws are white with wide red bands. Long white antennae. Pale blue eggs are often visible.
Head sparsely covered in small pores. Finger-like nasal cirrus. 2nd spot on dorsal fin far back, not touching base.
Short snout. Pale irregular body bars and sometimes a thin brown central line running the length of the body.
Translucent body with eight reddish-brown bands. Large smooth major claw. Often found living in pairs.
Long banded tentacles surrounding a white oral disc. Central tuft of short pale to lavender tentacles. Nocturnal.
Green to gray with distinctively banded arms. Central disc solid green, speckled or with bold irregular patterns.
Grayish-brown body. Distinctive row of black spots from chin to tail base. Pale abdomen. Two black bars on tail.
Coppery-orange body with broad black bars. Dark spots and bands on wide pectorals. Three black bars on tail
Brown body with a silvery sheen near abdomen. Bright orange crescent above iris. Small reddish pectoral fins.
Gray dorsal fin with large black spot. Dark lower lobe on tail. Bluish-black pectoral fin with a pale diagonal bar.
Bluish-gray body. Iridescent blue stripes below eye. Builds burrows in silty bottoms. Florida/W Caribbean.
Pale brown body with three brown stripes. Black spot on first dorsal fin, bordered in white. Schools over drop-offs.
Silvery with bluish upper body. Dark blue to black stripe from snout, over upper body and onto lower tail lobe.
Smooth elongated shell with a long thin spire. Dark brown to reddish. Mantle and proboscis are yellow.
Purple body and claws with red highlights. Blue eyes with distinctive horizontal black bars and tufts of bristles.
Translucent yellow body and orange head. Internal dark bar behind pectoral. Dark blotch near front of dorsal fin.
Azooxanthellate. Found in slope (Ref. 116012).
Reddish-brown head with orange spots. Dark bars are thinner and paler near abdomen. Found in Florida/Cuba.
Pale bronze body. Dark bar from behind second dorsal fin down to anal fin. A second dark bar at tail base.
Dark bar under eye and from nape to pectoral. Wide dark bar below front of dorsal fin. Small blue spots on snout.
distinctive_features: Body shape: short and / or deep

description: Pale body with orange to dusky markings. Dark bar at tail base. Black blotches on iris broken into irregular spots.
Gold to reddish body with white highlights. Banded legs. Wide band on tail. Found on Giant Basket Stars (p. 146).
Smooth bright red carapace with white spots. Legs have hints of purple and yellow at the joints. Dark claw tips.
Rounded fins, not connected at the rear. Short arms. Large chromatophores, commonly in red and orange.
Flattened oral disc with small warty projections in radiating lines. Numerous short pointed tentacles.
Twenty arms, usually golden. Side branches are tinged with silver and often have black tips. Hides in crevices.
Bulbous flexible body with yellow stripes on the sides. Ring of long pale feeding tentacles around the mouth.
Even very small juveniles of just a few millimeters are armed with widely spaced tufts of stinging bristles.
Elongated brown body with broad head. Thick branching barbels around chin. Large blue eyes. Belize to Panama.
Blue spots on top of head form lines on snout. Thin blue edge on anal fin. Black spot outlined in blue on dorsal fin.
distinctive_features: Body shape: short and / or deep

description: Bright orange shell with a sharply pointed spire. Bands of small black markings. Single white band on final whorl.
Pink body with orange abdomen. Three dark bars: behind second dorsal fin, near tail and at tail base.
Reddish-brown. Black bars onto dorsal fin. Dark stripe through eye. Dark spots on tail. Florida/Gulf of Mexico.
Flat body with pale contractile tentacles from either end. Color matches substrate, with pink or brown highlights.
Silvery oval body. Anal fin and tail not aligned. Similar species: Darkfin Chub (K. bigibbus) has darker fins.
Smooth and rounded translucent body. One large major claw. Globular black eyes. Found hiding in tube sponges.
Black body with white blotches. One short round tail and one longer thin tail. Orange lines on head and parapodia.
A common variation of S. partitus: white body with darker dorsal and anal fins, tail base and tail. Yellow pectoral fin.
Body may be paler with tiny white speckles. Same thin white margin with irregular white lines pointing inwards.
Two non-parallel horns on rostrum. Postero-lateral spine has two lobes (compare to pointed on M. trispinosum).
Uniformly red to silvery-pink body. May have darker red bar or spots beneath eye. Schools over deeper reefs.
Silvery-blue body. Large eyes (larger than snout length). Mostly pelagic, but may form dense inshore schools.
Translucent salmon to bronze with paler abdomen. Indistinct dark stripe from snout, through eye to gill cover.
Occasionally the branches of the Bipinnate Sea Plume will be pale pink, yellow or even dull whitish in color.
Jet-black body with two small lobes on the tail, one slightly longer than the other. May show thin white lines.
Forty long black arms. Numerous short black side branches, each tipped in white. Short curved legs.
Dark brown with short snout and overhanging mouth. Black vertical fins fused with tail. May act as a cleaner.
Tiny individuals under a common globular black tunic. Protruding siphon edges may be speckled with white.
Distinctive hump on the back of the shell. Attach to Black Corals (p. 90). Often encrusted with a layer of sponge.
Transparent body with a thin red stripe. Widely spaced eyes on long eyestalks. Found on black corals (p. 90).
Black with white patches around eye and outer edge of rhinophores. Black cerata with white spots. White foot.
Dark blue to black with pale blue-white lines along base of dorsal and anal fins. Blue lines radiate above eye.
distinctive_features: Body shape: short and / or deep

description: Grayish-brown to black body with darker rectangular patches. Thin pale margins on pectoral fin and tail.
Black, gray or dark brown body and fins. May display tinges of blue. Pectoral fins are usually solid black.
distinctive_features: Body shape: short and / or deep

description: Silvery to olive body with steep forehead. Spot on upper gill cover. Dark scutes to tail base. Found on deep reefs.
Intertidal, on muddy bottoms (Ref. 83910).
Large diffuse black patch behind pectoral fin. All fins are black. Found in or near the entrances to caves.
distinctive_features: Body shape: short and / or deep

description: Colony made up of many tiny individuals in a common swollen black tunic. Scattered pale incurrent siphons.
It develops a chemical defense against other invertebrates from the high concentration of prostaglandins in its soft tissues (Ref. 100527).
Gray to bluish-black. White spots on scales form stripes. Thin blue lines on cheek in juveniles. Found in Florida.
Solid dark blue body and fins. High, curving lateral line. Found on deeper reefs and headlands with current.
Solid black body and siphons with no distinctive markings. May be paler when growing under ledges.
Long dark red to black body. Often a pale stripe along the side. Found on Long Spine Sea Urchins (p. 149).
Uniformly black body and pseudotentacles. Light gray underside. Found mostly in sandy areas. Circumtropical.

Maximum depth reported taken from Ref. 128739. Common on shallow reefs, seagrass beds (Ref. 415) and hard bottoms. Also found in mangroves (Ref. 86836). Occasionally on deeper muddy sand bottoms. Often emits a strong, sulfurous pungent scent when removed from the water (Ref. 85482). In Belize, it was found in a submarine cave near Columbus Cay (Ref. 87209). Inhabits coralline algae reefs, coral communities, and lower mesophotic reefs (Ref. 128739).
Brown with white banding and dark spots. Radioles form a distinctive horseshoe shape. Tube is hidden in the reef.
Solid white dorsum with pointed gray tubercles, each circled in black. Black-tipped rhinophores.
Distinctive black bar at edge of gill cover to pectoral base. Thin white margins on all fins except the pectoral.
Purple body with dark patch from snout to dorsal. Often thin gold lines on head. Usually deeper than G. loreto.
Blocky markings with stripes over head, or rarely a solid yellow-green. Bright yellow eye. Found in seagrass beds.
Long sloping snout. Tall first dorsal fin is usually white. Five oblique body bars equally dark, last is wider at top.
Tall first dorsal fin. Orange-ringed bluish ocellus on rear dorsal fin. Branched single stalk cirrus over eye and nape.
Bronze body and dark speckles on fins. Enlarged pelvic fins, often yellowish and with a darker leading edge.

Observed to mainly perch on live coral surfaces and terminal phase males in holes, also typically within live coral heads. It has been reported to be widely distributed down to 20 m in Cayman Islands (Ref. 125603).
Pale gray to light brown body. Yellow patch on upper tail base and a yellow tail. Black spot at base of pectoral fin.
Each segment has a black line surrounded by white, forming a line down the back. Found in sandy areas.
Brown to brick-red carapace with darker mottling. Red stripes on walking legs. Claws banded with red to black.
Pale brown body with reddish spots with black centers. Small black spots on face and head. Protruding nostrils.
Dark markings on all fins except for the white anal fin. Distinctive white streak on side and a white abdomen.
In shallow reef habitats with surge or more flow that A. alcicornis habitats (Ref. 415). Colonial (Ref. 19). Known from mangroves (Ref. 86642).
Elongated claw arms, much wider than carapace, with distinctive sharp forward-facing teeth on the outer edge.
Encrusting calcified layer of larger pink to reddish zooids, visible to the naked eye. Often found at depth.
Irregular flattened body column, covered in large pale bulbous projections (vesicles) when tentacles retracted.
Tall erect stalks covered in triangular leaves with distinct rounded swellings in the centers. Found in the shallows.
Bluish-gray to light brown carapace covered with dark brown lines and blotches. Sharp spines on claw arms.
Yellow snout, front abdomen and pelvic fin. Middle body bar is straight (compare to all curved bars in H. ciliaris).
distinctive_features: Body shape: short and / or deep

description: Rounded and flattened leaf with pale filamentous edges. Thin bright green or blue bands. Forms small clusters.
Bright blue to purplish body. Darker upper body from snout to tail base. Deeply forked tail with black borders.
The largest of the swimming crabs. Olive carapace with blue on all legs and claws. Females have orange claws.
Zooxanthellate (Refs. 116012, 121684). On rocky reef of shallow waters (Ref. 121684).
Similar to Hovering Dartfish (below), but with a long pointed tail in adults and black or dark red margins on dorsal fin.
Bright blue head, body and front of dorsal and anal fins. Clear tail with black borders. Found in Florida and Cuba.
Uniformly light blue body with gray area on cheek. All fins are blue. Distinctively blunt head. Small pale blue eyes.
Silvery-blue with olive upper body. Small dark spot on upper gill cover. Tail lobes with distinctively black tips.
Translucent body with dark green lines. Inflated white-tipped cerata. Found on Green Bubble Weed (p. 19).
Bright yellow circular body with bright blue margins on dorsal and anal fins. Gold iris circled in bright blue.
distinctive_features: Body shape: short and / or deep

description: Flattened claws of equal size. Small blue eyes on red eyestalks (compare to gold eyestalks on P. erythrops).
Pale brown body and dark, banded legs and claw arms. Distinctive blue on eyestalks, antennae and mouth-parts.
Parapodia with tufts of bristles line the sides. Pale blue iridescent stripes. Some describe as variation of H. picta.
Opaque, blue to purple body. Siphon rims are paler than the rest of the body. Commonly found in large clusters.
Prominent wide orange bars. Blue-gray interspaces with midline thin white lines. Two orange bars below eye.
Dark upper body with yellow stripe. White abdomen. Large black spot at front of dorsal fin. Other fins clear.
Reddish-brown body, paler near abdomen. Pale blue on mouth and front of anal fin. Blue pelvic fins. Orange iris.
distinctive_features: Body shape: short and / or deep

description: Thin pink stripes from eye to snout. Pink spots behind gill cover continue into a wide pink stripe to tail base.
Greenish body with blue spots on closed parapodia and on rhinophores. White papillae on parapodia and foot.
Elongated body with rows of pale blue spots. Tail with long trailing filament. Often found in shallow seagrass.
Bright blue stripes on a yellowish body. Black tail and rear dorsal fin. Pectoral, pelvic and anal fins are yellow.
Juveniles have smaller pectorals and a distinctive dark spot at rear of first dorsal fin. Spot at center of tail base.
Clusters of pink to creamy-red cylindrical branches. Branches are smooth and elongated with sparse hairs.
Five short triangular arms with blunt tips. Color varies from white to yellow, red or blue. Found among rubble.
Smooth central disc in solid gray or black. Arms have prominent blunt spines, becoming shorter near arm tips.
Zooxanthellate (Ref. 116012).
Metallic bluish-green upper body with dark stripes. Yellow snout. Two dorsal fins are connected near base.
Transparent body with four wing-like flaps. Internal organs visible at night. Distinct lobes gather in towards the mouth.
Long silvery to greenish body with a single tall dorsal fin. Dark oval spot on pointed snout. Found in shallow areas.
Metallic yellow-green upper body with dark stripes. Orange snout. Two dorsal fins are widely separated.
Robust light to dark gray body with a paler abdomen. Short rostrum. Coastal individuals are usually smaller.
Zooxanthellate (Ref. 116012). Common throughout the Caribbean on reef tops (Ref. 415).
Zooxanthellate (Ref. 116012).
Slippery rose-red flattened stalks, repeatedly branching to form a low bush. Found attached to deeper substrates.
Small brownish knobs on thin branching finger-like tentacles. Often with pale brown to white highlights.
Rounded clumps of single-celled tubular branchlets, narrow at the base and branching out near the top.
Clumps of short cylindrical reddish-brown stalks. May have iridescent bluish-green bands near tips (above).
Tall reddish-brown stalks with short fluffy alternating tufts of branchlets. Found in protected areas on deeper reefs.

Branchlets alternate at right angles from a single stalk. Tiny polyps are found on both sides of the branchlets. Thick reddish-brown central stalk. Branchlets in a single plane with alternating polyps. Often found in clusters.
Oval to kidney-shaped calcified green blades growing in a single plane. Prefers shallow waters near mangroves.

Maximum depth reported taken from Ref. 128739. Common in reefs (Ref. 85482) and sea grass (Ref. 415). Also found in mangroves (Ref. 86836). Hosts alpheid shrimps in its canals (Ref. 86626). Inhabits coral communities. Found in shallow coral reefs and mesophotic depths (Ref. 128739).

Common on reefs (Ref. 415) and hard bottoms where coral is not dense in depths between 2 to 70 m (Ref. 85482). Also found in mangroves (Ref. 86836). In Belize, it was found in a submarine cave near Columbus Cay (Ref. 87209).
Silvery-blue with thin brassy to gold body stripes. Rear edge of anal fin aligned with top leading edge of tail.
distinctive_features: Body shape: short and / or deep

description: Distinctive red reticulated markings under arms. When startled the eye is white with a dark bar through the iris.

Short grayish-blue bulbous tentacles, often with paler tips. Translucent pedal disc. Shallow reefs to intertidal. Pale tentacles become maroon towards the mouth. The oral disc has large pale blotches. Shallow reefs or sand. An undescribed species. Anemones in this genus are characterized by having swollen tips on the tentacles.
Pale orange with white spots on both head and dorsum. Lamellate rhinophores, shorter than the oral tentacles.
Loose clusters of thin tan to gold branches that divide near the tips. Found in the shallows among Turtle Grass.
Red to salmon body with no distinctive markings. Usually a dark line radiating from eye. Found near anemones.
Elongated dark patch above pectoral fin and another at base of dorsal fin. Spots on body and tail (not on fins).
distinctive_features: Body shape: short and / or deep

description: As in C. tortugae but with dark X markings along flank, two dark spots at tail base. Prefers shallow silty areas.
A rounded ball shape of tightly packed filaments growing from a stout green stalk. Often a darker shade of green.
Red to orange carapace with two pale lines towards the rear. Claw arms and legs bristled with short brown hairs.
Narrow dark bar behind second dorsal fin and wide bar towards tail base, each with a narrow white bar behind it.
Brown oral disc with a whitish central mouth. Tentacles of different lengths. Common in lagoons and mangroves.
Yellow on tips on dorsal fin and tail lobes. Small white spot behind dorsal fin. Large dark spot at pectoral base.

Found around exposed seaward reefs of isolated high islands such as the northernmost Mariana and Bonin Is. Feeds on Sargassum and Turbinaria algae. Valued as a food fish, but not in Hawaii (Ref. 3921).
distinctive_features: Body shape: short and / or deep

description: Dark brown body with white eyes, eyestalks and tail. Found only on Black and White Crinoids (p. 147).
Purplish-brown dorsum with tiny tubercles, rhinophores and gills of same color. Rigid mantle. Feeds on sponges.

Maximum depth reported taken from Ref. 128739. Occurs on coral reefs, hard bottoms, lagoons (Ref. 85482), and mangroves (Ref. 86836). It hosts alpheid shrimps in its canals (Ref. 83410). Inhabits coralline algae reefs, coral communities, algal nodules, shallow coral and lower mesophotic reefs (Ref. 128739).
Smooth-walled lumpy reddish-brown to tan encrusting sponge. Scattered excurrent openings with paler rims.
Brown fan-shaped colony of flattened branching stalks with distinctively red tips. Often under darker ledges.
Alternating brown and white bands on a circular crown of radioles. The tube below is usually hidden in the reef.
Dark head with pale spots and white abdomen. Found in burrows, forming large colonies on deeper sand patches.
Prominent hump on the back. Often two ocellated spots on lower body. Found in the shallows and on seagrass.
Smooth flattened brown valves with dark gray to black mantle. Found in clumps on mangrove roots and docks.
Translucent body with bands of tiny brown speckles across the back. A commercially harvested species.
Pale brown tentacles around a darker brown oral disc. The body is white. Found on deeper sponge species.
Coral reefs, usually below 10 m in depth (Ref. 415). Also occurs in mangrove ponds (Ref. 86789).
Common on coral reefs, reef flats, seagrass beds and lagoons. Burrows in dead corals. Sporadic shallow excavations on live corals (Ref. 85482). Also in mangrove ponds (Ref. 86789).
Pale gray dorsum with black blotches. Elevated rows of white tubercles. Gills and rhinophores match body color.
White dorsum covered in tiny papillae. Rhinophores and gills are pale. Tiny white glands around mantle edges.
Greenish-brown upper body and creamy white below. Often green blotch behind gill cover, onto pectoral base.
Tan to greenish-gray body, sometimes with light blue spots. Distinctive rounded hump on back. Elongated tail base.
distinctive_features: Body shape: short and / or deep

description: Low chains of calcified bead-like spheres with no visible stalk in between. May attach to sand or rocky substrates.
Tall heavy body. Dorsal fin begins above the middle of pectoral fin. Small eye. Upper lobe of tail is the largest.
Deep red encrusting alga. Smooth surface, often shiny. Outer edges may not touch substrate, often overlapping.
Orange body with pointed orange and black cerata. Black rhinophores with pale blue or white posteriors.
A single stalk branches and re-branches, forming a fan shape. Bright red with white polyps. Found under ledges.
Uniformly pale yellow body. Large black spot or saddle at tail base. Black spot on snout (sometimes absent).
distinctive_features: Body shape: short and / or deep

description: Rounded pale brown to yellow valves with faint growth lines. Pale interior with numerous short white tentacles.
Small rounded clusters of tunicates gathered around a common excurrent siphon. Typically orange or purple.
Intertidal; on muddy bottoms (Ref. 83910).
Thin rounded fan-shaped blades growing outward in a single plane from a common stalk. Green to yellowish.
Short bushy tufts of upright branches from low runners. Small irregular branchlets give a rough appearance.
Coppery stripes down a pale silvery body. Dark pelvic, rear dorsal and anal fins. Black tail. Bright blue eyes.
Flattened oval shell with alternating large and small ribs and finely toothed margin. Mantle color highly variable.
Orange with purple stripes. Black ocellus on rear dorsal fin. Ocellated spots on tail not joined (compare: L. rubre).
Spherical bell with a short skirt of tentacles below. Red, yellow or bluish markings, mostly around the rim.
Oval pink to reddish body with large eye near front of head. Pale fins. Distinctively rounded lobes on tail.
distinctive_features: Body shape: short and / or deep

description: Reddish to dusky head and body. Pointed snout. Single long banded cirrus. Very long filamentous pelvic fins.
Orange-brown shell with small pointed spire. Final whorl with row of short spikes. Dark stripe on brown tentacles.
Brownish body with darker blotches. Large white spots, denser along medial line. White-tipped pseudotentacles.
Blue stripe through eye, from snout onto tail. Cleaner species. W Caribbean. Similar species: E. oceanops in Florida/Cuba.
Reddish with white reticulated pattern on head, body and claw arms. Found inside living pen shells (p. 215).
Distinctive white cross on frontal region, between eyes. Outer edge of claws bristled. Brownish bands on legs.
Smooth plates, often red with white spots. Rusty girdle, characteristically without overlapping scales. Uncommon.
Green to bluish body mottled with brown; may change color rapidly. Distinctive dark brown ring around the eye.
The most common squid. Thin unbroken fin runs along the entire length of the body. Color is highly changeable.
Gray requiem shark with a white abdomen; first dorsal starts well behind the pectorals. Dusky undersides on pectoral and pelvic tips help separate it from bull shark.
distinctive_features: Dorsal behind pectorals; dusky fin tips; white belly
abundance: Common on Caribbean reefs and drop-offs (Bahamas, Belize, Bay Islands, Florida Keys).
habitat_behavior: Patrols mid-water and reef edges; often encountered on walls and shark dives.
diver_reaction: Stay calm and horizontal; give space at cleaning stations — do not chase or block escape routes.

description: Dusky gray. Dorsal fin starts above pectoral edge. 2nd dorsal fin above middle of anal fin. Found in shallows.
Pale orange to dark red dorsum and thickened mantle edges (only when disturbed). Red rhinophores and gills.
Reddish carapace with large white spots on abdomen. Black and white horns above eyes. Long spiny antennae.
Pale brown carapace, sometimes with a darker longitudinal stripe. Dark-banded claw arms. Found in sand and rubble.
Two distinctive dark blue ocellated spots near the eyes. These will disappear when the octopus is camouflaged.
Thick-walled shell with a small pointed conical spire. Mantle is brown and speckled. Often covered in algae.
Circular gray body with large gill openings behind eyes. Snout and tips of fins are rounded. Inshore shallows.
Zooxanthellate (Ref. 116012). Known from a submarine cave at a depth of 10 m (Ref. 87209).
Almost flattened spire with a short pointed spire. Fine shallow grooves cover final body whorl. Red mantle.
White stripe running down dorsum. Thin white ring on tips of cerata. Found on Upsidedown Jellies (p. 110).
White patch on head extends onto short rhinophores. Parapodial edge is paler with a thin solid brown line.
Uniformly red body. Black bar on tail, edged in white. Black spot on tip of rear dorsal fin. Yellow patch on snout.
Calcified green blade with a felt-like texture, as wide as it is tall. Narrow stalk. Often found in deeper sandy areas.
Silvery to greenish-blue body. Rows of spots above and below a broken orange midbody stripe. Dark dorsal fin.
Dark body with bright yellow markings of irregular spots and bars, forming a chain-like pattern. Found in shallows.
Elongated snout with a dark stripe. Bright patch on gill cover. Thin dark bars along body. Florida/Gulf of Mexico.
Bluish-gray to pinkish-brown with darker bars across upper body. Pale blotches on side. Translucent fins.
Only the male has very large claws, often longer than width of carapace. Brick-red above, white on abdomen.
Eyes are distinctively close together. Two dark spots on lateral line: under laid-down pectoral and at base of tail.
Mottled brown with three dark saddles. Two rows of dark blotches form a checkerboard pattern on lower flank.
Inhabits rocky and coral reefs, often inside tube sponges (Ref. 9710).
Pale cream body with dark patches and spots. White abdomen. Bright orange eyes. Shallow sandy areas.
Dark blue body. Yellow to orange face. Blue ring around eye. Bright blue margins on fins. Often on deeper reefs.
distinctive_features: Body shape: short and / or deep

description: Dark brown shell with knobbed ribs and a single thin white spiral cord. Deep red mantle with white speckles.
Reddish-brown body. Rows of white spots on upper and lower jaws. Only tips of jaws are touching. Yellow iris.
Shiny mustard-yellow to greenish-yellow sponge, often with dark blotches. Pale radiating veins around openings.
Olive-brown on top. White below, becoming gray at rear edges. Mouth on underside (compare: M. aff. birostris).
Pointed conical shell with thin chocolate-brown lines. Similar brown lines on beige mantle. Dark brown tentacles.
Branchlets alternate from a single stalk. White polyps on tips of each branchlet and stalk. Often found in clusters.
Two spiraling crowns of radioles. Usually found in contrasting shades of purple, white and orange.
Brown to light green body with white spots or stripes. Can change colors and pattern quickly as it moves.
The central disc has ten spots, each outlined in black, arranged around the rim. Arms are usually banded.
Brownish body with bright gold spots circled in black. Long transparent legs and antennae. Transparent tail.
Smooth rounded cream to tan body with brown blotches and small spots (darker around short pointed papillae).
Red areas on dorsum do not extend to mantle edge. Patterns for this species are more varied than F. binza.
Two rows of irregular brown blotches. White lines under eye. White bar behind pectoral. Florida/Gulf of Mexico.
Large dark midbody blotch. Yellow face with red lines on cheek and around eye. Colors and patterns may vary.
Bulbous translucent bell and numerous thin tentacles. Distinctive club-shaped organs where tentacles attach.
Common in shallow water in Bocas del Torro, Panama (Ref. 415). In Belize, it was found in a submarine cave near Columbus Cay at a depth of 10 m (Ref. 87209).
Flattened disc, sometimes flecked with pale spots. Large bulbous tips on tapering tentacles. Variable in color.
Dark body, often with yellow abdomen, anal fin and tail. Dark scale borders form lines. Often a dark spot at tail base.
distinctive_features: Body shape: short and / or deep

description: Pale grooved shell with dark patches. Brownish mantle covering the shell may have many knobby protrusions.
Flattened oral disc with bulbous warty projections. Tentacles around rim formed into rectangular shapes.
As in C. tortugae but with small brown flecks, spots on top of head, pair of colon-like spots on pectoral base.
Rounded shell with thin brown bands. White mantle with reddish-brown markings: lines in front and spots at the rear.
Found on bay and nearshore, and outer continental shelf (Ref.109199).
Translucent pear-shaped shell showing the animal’s markings underneath. Common in sandy shallow areas.
Four to seven rounded arms with blunt tips. All arms can be of different lengths. Color varies from orange to tan.
Uniformly dark under arms (compare: O. insularis). Color and patterns variable. Eye is dark when startled (above).
Minimum depth from Ref. 109199. Intertidal (Ref. 109199).
Long sloping forehead and a distinctively darkened lateral line. Found in shallow lagoons and mangroves.
Pink to dark red, often with a broad white stripe. White- banded legs and claw arms. Distinctively short claws.
Black to bronze with black speckles. Row of black dots down mid-side. Dark lines behind eye. Black pelvic fins.
Translucent gray with brown and white spots. Few long and very bulbous cerata. Bands on smooth rhinophores.
Light brown with dark upper body. White spots above tail base and often below dorsal fin. Black spots on lower lip.
Dark brown with numerous lighter cone-shaped podia along the back, circled in light brown. The sole is reddish.
Five tubular arms with rows of red to orange or pale brown conical spines. Maroon color between the spines.
Maximum depth from Ref. 108813. Deeper parts of reefs; greater than 5 m deep (Ref. 415).
Maximum depth reported taken from Ref. 128739. Inhabits coralline algae reefs, algal nodules, and lower mesophotic reefs (Ref. 128739).
Squat reddish-orange body covered with fine hairs. Found deep in crevices, caves and on deeper reefs.
Azooxanthellate. Found in the outer continental shelf (Ref. 116012).
Wide red carapace and bristled legs. Numerous conical white spines on claw arms. Blunt spines around eyes.
Flattened spiny carapace and white-banded legs. Found living within pits and galls on a variety of coral species.
Elongated yellowish body with four or five faint body bars. Dorsal and anal fins pale, darkening towards tail.
distinctive_features: Body shape: short and / or deep

description: Pink to bright red on a pale background. Dark saddle above pectoral fin. Tail with red bar and red margin.
Numerous transparent tentacles have a spiraling white pattern. Oral disc usually hidden. Found in sandy areas.

Maximum depth from Ref. 109199. This species is found at depths of 0.5 to 12 m, in coral reefs and rocky bottoms (Ref. 83916). Intertidal up to a depth of 55 m (Ref. 91956). In Belize, it was found in a submarine cave near Columbus Cay at a depth of 10 m (Ref. 87209).
Rounded silvery to dusky body, sometimes with faint bars. Low triangular dorsal fin and tail, often darker gray.
Wide black stripe runs from dorsal onto black tail, which is edged in white. Faint yellow body stripes. Pale fins.
distinctive_features: Body shape: short and / or deep

description: Long thin fleshy barbels around chin, rarely branching. Large whitish nostrils in front of eyes. Yucatan to Belize.
Clumps of dark green elongated bubbles, randomly branching with bulbous tips. Found in sheltered areas.
Smooth fleshy mats of round creeping and overlapping lobes. Dark green and covered in short fine pale hairs.
Dark purple head and upper body. Rear abdomen, tail base and anal fin yellowish-orange. Light purple tail.
Color varies from red to purple. Yellowish abdomen. Red spot at pectoral base. Usually on outer edges of reefs.
Males with raised fleshy crest along midline of head. Dark stripes behind eye. Shallow coasts and mangroves.
Short upturned snout. Brown body with darker broken stripes. Transparent tail. Usually found in areas of algae.
Brown dorsum with white spots. Banded pointy cerata. Rhinophores with knobby tubercles. Short oral tentacles.
Large blunt head. Silvery body with yellowish anal fin and tail. High lateral line. Black spot on upper gill cover.
Long thin arms with light banding and numerous short spines. Arms extend only at night, disc remains hidden.
Bright red body curving upwards near the tail. Large red claws with pale tips. Found in areas of sand and rubble.
Pink to translucent dorsum with a fine network of white to yellow reticulated lines. Short yellow branching cerata.
Transparent body with thin dark brown lines and bands. Found in shallows on mangrove roots and under docks.
Translucent body with white and brown spots. Elongated transparent cerata with pale cross-like markings at tips.
Small translucent dome with eight thin tentacles, half the width of the dome, with round yellow bulbs at each base.
Light brown rounded oval scales with a rough texture from low tubercles. Pale cirri and dark feeding tentacles.
Bushy clumps of thin fan-shaped green leaves with distinctly pointed three-lobed blades. Found in crevices.
Elongated thick-walled dark red leaves. Blade edges are rough and crenelated. Found growing in small clusters.
Dark brown bars edged with blue or blue spots. Second and third bars are an inverted Y. Thin dark bars from eye.
Triangular red carapace, covered in red sponge and often small zoanthids. Purplish claw arms with darker spots.
Short flattened body with widely spaced lobes and visible musculature. Comb rows run perpendicular to the gut.
Red to black body with blue spots and faint orange lines. Distinctive white patch on front of head and at midbody.
Elongated ribbed white shell with thin dark bands above each whorl. Often a broader dark band on the final whorl.
The largest of the snappers. Heavy body with large head and thickened lips. May display contrasting body bars.
Distinctive upward curve on sharp rostrum. Pale pink body with small orange spots. Often in tube sponges.
Five short triangular arms. Thick reddish-orange body with a network of raised red bumps in radiating lines.
Radial canals extend onto a large belly, half of the bell’s diameter. Sparse thin tentacles, swollen at the base.
Brown to greenish body with pale spots. Greenish-blue edges on abdominal plates. Colorful club-shaped claws.
Flattened reddish carapace with a distinctively wide front between the eyes. Banded legs. Found in the shallows.
Tan body with numerous thin dark brown longitudinal stripes. Row of white spots runs down medial line.
Dark bar through eye onto cheek. Row of dark midlateral dashes. Males have a dark patch on the lower gill cover.
A bushy growth of rounded dark green branchlets, often covered in very fine hairs. Usually found in the shallows.
Found on bay and nearshore, and slope (Ref. 109199).
Reddish-brown body. Black blotch at rear edge of first dorsal fin. White bar on tail base and red bar onto tail.
Pink to bright red plates of varying thickness, formed into rounded floral shapes. Only found on deeper reefs.
Transparent body with short tentacles up to 30 cm / 1 ft. Small individuals may show a greenish bioluminescence.
Clear bell with four radial canals. Thick primary tentacles surrounded by numerous thinner marginal tentacles.
Found on bay and nearshore, and outer continental shelf (Ref. 109199).
Dense clusters of extremely fine calcified branches. Pale pink to yellowish-green, with distinctively white tips.
Flattened gray to pinkish carapace with highly variable markings. Numerous blunt spines protruding from sides.
Long whip-like stalk, tapering slightly towards the tip. Red to orange with white polyps. Found on deeper reefs.
Yellow head with pointed snout. Ocellated blue spot on first dorsal fin. Usually found near Giant Anemones (p. 116).
A long tubular mass of tiny purple eggs encased in a transparent jelly. The squid itself is from very deep water.
Colonial (Ref. 19). Known from deeper coastal substrates (25- 100m) of the offshore fore-reef slope and Banks (Ref. 86643).
Elongated bell with visible golden brown branching canals. Long thin tentacles retracted when swimming.
Zooxanthellate (Ref. 116012). Found in shallow environments (Ref. 847).
Brick-red to pale orange. Upper valve with radial ribs and long flattened spines, lower valve cemented to the reef.
Bright red to orange with prominent barred pattern, bars become stripes to rear. Dark zigzag bars over abdomen.
A series of darker curved bars (may be faint) start behind pectoral fin, which is edged in a darker blue.
distinctive_features: Body shape: short and / or deep

description: Olive to bronze with a distinctive white triangle under eye. Often blue spots on gill cover. May show faint body bars.
Metallic bluish-green with golden abdomen. Males have distinctively pronounced forehead. Found in open ocean.
Light brown to black with numerous creases along the body. The sole is red to white with tiny brown parapodia.
Slender light brown body with dark saddles. Large dark spots along the lateral line. Distinctive tiny oval pupil.
Maximum depth from Ref. 109199. It is found on coral reefs and rocky bottoms at depths of 4 to 10 m (Ref. 83916).
Reddish head, usually with irregular patches of orange. Dark bars extend onto very spotted dorsal and anal fins.
The color of the digestive tracts in the cerata varies from red to bright orange or yellow, sometimes as a thin band.
Faint bars and heavy dark/light speckling. Distinctive ocellated blue spot on upper rear body, onto dorsal fin.
Pink with single dark spots on scales. Spots on head are often larger. Dark bar under eye. Bar at tail base.
Dorsal and anal fins extend only to the end of the tail base. Thin dark border on lower edge of the anal fin.
distinctive_features: Body shape: short and / or deep

description: Bars and small pores on jaw. Finger-like nasal cirrus. Adults: no dorsal fin spots, juveniles: one spot near front.
Brownish-red with wide silvery stripes and thinner dark stripes. White leading edge on pelvic and anal fins.
Inhabits patch reefs and coral heads in relatively deep water (Ref. 5521).
The smallest of the frogfishes. Small brown spots on first dorsal soft ray and at mid-base of dorsal fin. Short lure.
distinctive_features: Body shape: short and / or deep

description: Reddish to orange body with no bars. Rounded orange spots below eye. Males have long first dorsal spines.
Orange to yellow shell with a sharply pointed spire. Elongated aperture with a distinctively toothed lip.
Pale oval shell with distinctive beaded black ribs. Convex in front and concave behind the dark-rimmed aperture.
Small red to brown body with few papillae and broad banding on arms. Juveniles live in empty clam shells.
Pale, with dark stripes on upper body. Dark bar from snout, through eye to tail base. Blue markings on cheek.
The smallest member of this genus. Short spines and bushy cirrus. Dusky orange eyes. Found in shallows.
Smooth polished-looking valves. Cream to yellowish with orange or brown marks that may be zigzag, as above.
Only the oral disc appears above sand, with short round tentacles. Retracts into a donut shape during the day.
Translucent yellow to brown with dark spotting. Notched cephalic shield. Parapodia cover only the front of shell.
Maximum depth from Ref. 116012. Zooxanthellate (Ref. 116012). Found in shallow areas of high wave action, especially reef crests (Ref. 415) and submerged reef tops (Ref. 86439).
Flattened carapace edged in black, with tiny pale spots. Small black and white spots on claws. Red-tipped legs.
Pale green to white body. Parapodia lined with red. Red spot on short rhinophores. Distinctive blue eyeshadow.
Tall cylindrical stalks of light green, tipped with darker green feather-like branchlets. Grows in dense clumps.
Short dense dark green leaves growing upwards from a central stalk. More elongated than similar species.
A spreading mat of pale to olive-green rounded or oval bubble shapes. Distinctive silvery sheen on the surface.
Bright green to brownish body with white spots and lines. Dark lines radiate from eye. Seagrass beds, up to 3m.
Elongated emerald-green body with gray-pink patch on left of dorsum. Gray on rhinophores and oral tentacles.
Small bright green shell with white spots or bands. Long thin tentacles. Found on Turtle Grass, on which it feeds.
Robust domed shell with short knobs. Cream to tan. Vertical opening in the front. Lip is a solid cream color.
Variable thin fan-shaped blades. Shades of green to light brown. Often overlapping and covering large areas.

Maximum depth recorded from Belize (Ref. 87218) Inhabit a variety of reef environments throughout the Caribbean (Ref. 415), at depths of 0.5 to 12 m in coral reefs and rocky bottoms (Ref. 83916). Also found in Carrie Bow Cay, Belize in lagoon and back reef, reef crest, low spur and groove zone, outer ridge and fore-reef slope at depths of up to 25 m (Ref. 87218).
Tiny individual tunicates growing from a common tunic that spreads across the substrate. Color varies greatly.
Short tentacles arranged around a raised circular disc. Grows in loose clusters within a common fleshy base.
Widely spaced cerata with orange bands, connected by thick white lines. White-tipped rhinophores and tentacles.
Found in mangroves (Ref. 86836). Commensal with amphipods (Ref. 86735). In Belize, it was found in a submarine cave near Columbus Cay at a depth of 10 m (Ref. 87209).
Maximum depth from Ref. 108813. Common on mangrove roots (Ref. 415), shallow reefs, bays, lagoons, and seagrass beds on hard bottoms (Ref. 85482).
Covered in brown to tan rosettes. Dark midbody blotch. Males with orange spots between widely spaced eyes.
distinctive_features: Body shape: short and / or deep

description: Bulbous pointed green cerata with orange bands and black spots. Often a small blue spot above close-set black eyes.
Head and front of body purple, golden-yellow towards tail. Dark spot on front of dorsal fin. Dark stripe through eye.
Dark gray with a long tapering forehead. Sickle-shaped dorsal fin and long narrow flippers with pointed tips.
Brownish-gray body with translucent fins. Pale head with nostrils above upper lip. Tiny translucent pectoral fins.
Dirty yellow dorsum with brown patches. Low sheaths on dark rhinophores. Rows of white glands around mantle.
Thick oval to triangular, semi-glossy white valves. Thin gray growth rings and dark hinge usually visible.
Thick central stalks branch repeatedly to form a loose bush. Often covered in Hydroid Zoanthids (p. 127).
Thin tightly spaced branchlets alternate along a dark brown central stalk. The shape resembles a tall feather.
Translucent body. Distinctively long mobile filamentous dorsal spike. Endemic to the Bay Islands of Honduras.
Relatively large aperture. Distinctive concentric threads over the ribs, giving the appearance of a file’s surface.
Rounded tan to pinkish scales covered with fine hairs. Tufts of light brown bristles and pale cirri from under the scales.
Mantle has thin orange lines bordered in black, arranged in a fingerprint pattern. (Previously Cyphoma gibbosum.)

Maximum depth from Ref. 116013. Zooxanthellate (Ref. 121684). In reefs and seagrass beds and occasionally on mangrove roots. Very common in shallow protected areas in Bocas del Torro where there can be solid beds of this species (Ref. 415). Colonial (Ref. 19). Known from tidal inlets, sheltered areas of bays, including mangrove shores, exposed areas of bays, including any ledges at their entrance, sounds, shallow reefs and shallow coastal benthic habitats (< 25 m) (Ref. 86643).
Shallow reefs, mangroves, and seagrass beds. Very common in the Caribbean (Ref. 415). Commensal with amphipods (Ref. 82302, 86735).
Silvery body, slightly darker above. Distinctive black tips on front spines of first dorsal fin, with white area below.
Irregular wavy red lines on carapace form a pattern that dissolves towards the rear. Large claws cover the face.
Creamy white shell with two rows of knobs. Short spire. Wavy brown markings. Smaller than the King Helmet.
Dark blue body. Yellow to orange head, continuing onto nape and dorsal fin. Blue ring around eye. SE Caribbean.
distinctive_features: Body shape: short and / or deep

description: Dark spot under second dorsal fin. Stripe from snout to edge of gill cover. White lines on top and bottom of eye.
Red with thin white lines and a white bulls-eye on side of carapace. Banded legs and large claws of equal size.
Pale bulbous shell is wider in the middle. Creamy mantle has orange spots circled in black, often covering the shell.
Parapodia overlap, covering a small shell. Single siphon above head. Swims when disturbed, or buries into sand.
Dark green leaves with wide densely packed branchlets. Grows upwards from thick green runners on the sand.
Thin flattened valves and a dark brown interior mantle. Found on shallower reefs, often encrusted with sponges.
Long colonies of rust-colored or yellow to orange-red individuals, each sharing a larger excurrent siphon.
Distinctively flattened segments, repeatedly branching from a common base. Pink to reddish with white tips.
Thin leaves with toothed edges are sparsely distributed along a stalk attached to the reef. Fewer gas floats.
Tightly packed filaments end in a flat top, forming an inverted cone. Elongated stalk. Found in sandy areas.
Fleshy dark green leaves with rounded edges, often overlapping to form a thick tangle. Two-lobed branchlets.
Grows singly or in dense clusters. This corallimorph is also able to fluoresce, seeming to give off its own light.
Black markings visible through translucent rounded shell. Long tentacles with black stripes and a long clear foot.
Orange to brownish shell with pointed spines, always largest on final whorl. Aperture lip slants downwards.
Creased tapering body in brown, yellow or red. Often mottled. Numerous sharp podia along back and sides.
Pale circular body with brown blotches, straight at front of head. Short snout. Rounded dorsal fins set close to tail.
Dense clusters of twin-lobed dark brown blades edged in yellow, with yellow speckles. Can cover large areas.
Blunt snout and tapering body. Large wing-like pectoral fins extend to tail base, with bright blue spots and lines.
Flattened disc in a variety of colors, usually blotched. Small forked tentacles in lines radiating from the mouth.
Large black spot near tail base, circled in white. Thin chevron stripes. Yellow-edged black bar through eye.
distinctive_features: Body shape: short and / or deep

description: Long flattened segmented blades in pale to dark pink, often branching. Found growing from a common holdfast.
Small dense clusters of light green feather-shaped leaves with pointed tips. Thin pale green central stalk.
Maximum depth from Ref. 83917. Zooxanthellate (Ref. 116012).
Rows of dark crescent-shaped spots. Oblique dark bar down from eye. Dark bar on entire width of tail base.
Uniform dark brownish body with inconspicuous bars. Dark head with scattered small white spots. Dark fins.

Head with orange line and two leaf-like marks. Red rings on bulbous cerata. Bluish band at base of oral tentacles. Translucent to orange with dark cerata. White on head, white stripe down body. On hydroid P. disticha (p. 137).
Dark gray with yellow scale edges. Yellow around eye, at base of pectoral fin and on tips of dorsal and anal fins.
distinctive_features: Body shape: short and / or deep

description: Most juvenile grunts have dark stripes and tail base spot. Only the French Grunt has diagonal body stripes as well.
distinctive_features: Body shape: short and / or deep

description: Head with median orange line and two lateral blue lines. Dark jaw area. Cerata and oral tentacles with white tips.
Large dewlap on abdomen. Pronounced hump behind dorsal spine. Color varies, usually a white patch on side.
distinctive_features: Body shape: short and / or deep

description: Valve edges have a distinctive zigzag pattern. Often attached to gorgonians and encrusted with sponge.
Translucent white with a network of thin white lines. Similar markings on oral tentacles, head and long cerata.
Reddish tan with pointed lateral spines (often covered). Purplish claws. Often has Hitchhiking Anemone (p. 119).
Fleshy white to light brown body, often with blotches. Short podia on the back and in a skirt around the sole.
Zooxanthellate (Ref. 116012). Very shallow reef slopes and lagoon margins (Ref. 846).
A very dense ball of fine hair-like filaments. Gray, blue-green or burgundy on the outer layer and paler inside.
Tiny leaves in a delicate spiral pattern of light green with pale centers. Grows on long runners to form flat bushes.
Brown plates, often encrusted. Girdle with wide dark bands and short bristles. Found close to the waterline.
Short cylindrical white stalks with green tips. Grows in dense clusters, fused at the base. Tips appear fuzzy.
Pinkish-gray body. White, blue, yellow and red on cerata. White patch on front of head. Rhinophores fused at base.
Rounded bumpy orange to red carapace with yellow markings lined in black, also on legs and claw arms.
Mucilaginous mass of tiny yellow filaments. Attaches to and eventually overgrows substrates and living corals.
Clumps of light brown elongated undulating masses. Attaches to substrate or dead corals and spreads easily.
Rounded shell with a low spire. Spirals blotched in red and white over golden brown. Found in the shallows.
Tan to greenish body with long white lines or reticulated patterns. Rhinophores close-set, between parapodia.
The largest of the anemones. Dozens of long tentacles with blue, red or green bulbous tips. Orange or red pedal disc.
Elongated shell with a sharply pointed spire. Thin brown lines encircle the shell. White mantle. Buries into sand.

Maximum depth reported taken from Ref. 128739.. Maximum length refers to base diameter (Ref. 99323). Common on reef environments (Ref. 415). Also found in mangroves (Ref. 86836). Inhabits shallow coral reefs, coral communities, coralline algae reefs, algal nodules, and mesophotic reefs (Ref. 128739).
Long arms branch repeatedly from a tangled mass near the central disc. Light brown to reddish. Nocturnal.
The largest of the hermit crabs. Rough scaly texture on oversized claws. Long banded antennae. Pale eyestalks.
Globular body with two large siphons, often encrusted in algae. Incurrent siphon has thin inward-facing spines.
Tentacles are almost as long as the body, which is small. Translucent fins end in a point. Distinctively large eye.

Occurs in a wide range of substrates, where photographs show this species mostly on live coral surfaces of all morphologies, but also common on sponges, gorgonians, and dead hard bottoms; with TP males occupying holes in live corals, dead corals, and sponges (Ref. 125603). Inhabits globose coral heads of <i>Montastrea sp.<i> and sea fans (Ref. 51514).
Translucent orange with white segments along spinal column. Dark scale edges. Pepper spots on lower body.
Deep red to pale pink body, sometimes with silvery bars. Large red eyes. Dark specks on tail and dorsal/anal fins.
Tiny individuals empty into a common deep excurrent siphon. Thin pale siphon rims. Forms globular shapes.
Elongated pale orange cone, commonly with central broken band of brown spots on final whorl. Red mantle.
Elongated brown shell with large white patches and smaller white spots. Longer aperture lip than M. ocellata.
White to yellowish shell with white spots and blotches and faint bands. Translucent mantle with white blotches.
Reddish-orange. Long fleshy appendage on chin. Three blue ocelli on rear dorsal, outlined in yellow and then red.
Yellow dorsum with purple spots. Gold line on ruffled mantle edge. Brown-lined gills. Pale purple rhinophores.
Blue body with rows of grayish-blue cerata with orange spots. Long blue oral tentacles and short rhinophores.
Dark blue to black with yellow lines on dorsum. Yellow margin on white mantle edge, interrupted at midbody.
Golden to pale yellow body. Claws banded with white, red and orange. Red bands on tail. Long pale antennae.
Twenty to forty arms of either yellow, brown or black. Side branches are golden or black with golden tips.
Pale body with gold eyes and stripe on the back. Sides are transparent. Found only on Golden Crinoids (p. 147).
Head, body and fins golden-yellow. Black spot on snout, outlined in blue. Blue spots/lines around eye and cheek.
distinctive_features: Body shape: short and / or deep

description: Rounded golden-brown body with irregular darker brown spots and blotches. Small pointed papillae on the back.
Golden brown fluffy tufts growing from a central axis. Found growing on rocky substrates on deeper reefs.
Dark brown head and body covered in small yellow spots that become denser towards a yellow tail. Golden iris.
Three prominent dark anterior bars, often fusing to form a smudgy H shape. Lower head usually strongly barred.
Thick-walled shell with irregular knobby surface and rusty brown patches and dark lines. Heavily spotted mantle.
Blunt snout. Dark bar runs from top of head, through eye and onto cheek. Commonly a gold spot above pectoral.
Tan body with bright yellow spots inside diffuse black blotches, smaller towards snout. Protruding nostrils.
Zooxanthellate (Ref. 116012). Common in shallow less than 15 m; reef habitats and sometimes seagrass beds (Ref. 415). Also in intertidal rock pools and shallow reef environments (Ref. 848).
The largest of the groupers. Gray to greenish body with pale blotches. Small dark spots cover head and pectoral.
Elongated translucent body with red and white speckles and small hump. Found mostly on Sea Plumes (p. 101).
Maximum depth from Ref. 124095.
Long pointed brown shell with deep axial ridges. Pale mantle with irregular brown spots. Banded tentacles.
Dark body with white spots. White-rimmed sheaths on black rhinophores. Bulbous cerata. Found on hydroids (p. 136).
Rounded fins, not connected at the rear. Often displays greenish-yellow. Found in seagrass and shallow reefs.
Three yellow bars. Blue spots on pelvic and anal fins. Squared tail with transparent margin (compare: P. paru).
distinctive_features: Body shape: short and / or deep
abundance: Common on Caribbean and Florida reef slopes.
habitat_behavior: Often seen alone or in pairs over sponge and coral on mid-depth reefs.
diver_reaction: Usually keeps a polite distance; slow approaches work best for photos.

description: Uniformly gray to olive body with a sloping forehead. May display darker bar through eye. Rounded anal fin.
Dark gray body with bright blue spots and lines. Rarely on reefs, often found taking shelter under floating debris.
distinctive_features: Body shape: short and / or deep

description: Red spots on body and all fins. Four black or white spots below dorsal fin. Juveniles have white stripe on snout.
Silvery-gray upper body with oblique bars. Pointed snout and protruding lower jaw. Dorsal and anal fins close to tail.
Slightly notched snout, only slightly curved. Tall pointed dorsal fin. Small eye. Curved rear edge on pelvic fin.

Zooxanthellate (Refs. 116012, 121684). Found on rocky reef, in shallow to mesophotic waters (Ref. 121684). Common throughout the Caribbean to a depth of 70 m (Ref. 415). In Belize, it was found in a submarine cave near Columbus Cay at a depth of 10 m (Ref. 87209).
The largest of the jacks. Oblique dark bar from snout, through eye to dorsal fin base. Pale dorsal and anal fins.
Grayish-brown body with white blotches and a pointed snout. Rounded fins and tail. Often found lying on its side.
Flattened green bristed carapace and legs. Often holds strips of algae over head. Found on blade algae (p. 21).
Elongated dark green oval blades growing on runners. Short slender stalks. Commonly found on seagrass beds.
Light green bulbous shapes with small rounded knobs giving them a cobbled texture. Often found in clusters.
Knobby green carapace with green legs and claws. Red spot inside joints of claw arms. Claws tipped with white.
Tall erect feather-shaped leaves of light green, with fine branchlets. Grows from thin pale runners on the sand.
Reef; mangrove; seagrass environments (Ref. 415). On hard substrates, often hosting the zoanthid Parazoanthus swiftii (Ref. 85482). Commensal with amphipods (Ref. 82302).
Dense clumps of small green balls growing from pointed runners, giving the appearance of clusters of grapes.
Small clusters of green stalks, growing upward from the reef, with swollen rounded tips. Forms loose bunches.
Dark green cup shape on a long slender stalk. Rounded ridges radiate from the center to form bulbous edges.
The largest of the morays. Uniformly dark green body with no distinctive markings. Often under ledges in the daytime.
A fine network of thin tubular threads that interconnect. Pale to dark green. Can cover large areas of the reef.
Greenish-brown with thin dark transverse bands. Bristled legs and claws. Often found in lagoons and oyster beds.
Pink to reddish with dark edges on scales. Pale blue bars on lower head. Often a blue spot on dorsal fin. Red iris.
Distinctively smooth rounded shell. One pair of plates between the eyes (compare: Hawksbill’s two pairs).
Single greenish-yellow tube extending from the reef. Large incurrent siphon on a bulbous translucent tip.

Inhabits pitted limestone faces and tide pools in surf areas in clear waters. Usually found among spines of rock-boring sea urchins (Ref. 26938). A protogynous hermaphrodite (Ref. 55367). Has been reared in captivity (Ref. 35420).

Uniformly red body and tail. Pearly spots above pale abdomen. Red dorsal fin, yellow pelvic and anal fins. Greenish-brown upper body. Short blue bar at upper corner of gill cover. Dark spot at pectoral base. Red eye.
Flattened purple branches in fan or bush shapes. White polyps extend from grooves running down both edges.
Maximum depth from Ref. 116012. Zooxanthellate (Ref. 116012).
Bluish-white shell edged in orange. Short orange stalk. Found in clusters attached to floating debris and boats.
Five tubular arms, all of the same length. Each arm with a distinctively upturned red tip. Color is highly variable.
Usually dark brown. May be black, tan or greenish. Ocellus on gill cover is fully rounded with complete thin white ring.
Small dense bristles cover entire carapace and walking legs (last pair are longest for holding sponge in place).
Yellowish with brown spiral ribs and a large body whorl. Spotted mantle. Juveniles covered in hairy protrusions.
Short broadly rounded rostrum. Tubercles between eyes. Decorates with algae, commonly Halimeda. Reef areas.
Smooth light brown body with thin white stripes. Short widely spaced tufts of cerata. Long tentacles on oral veil.
Rough-edged light brown scales with rough tubercles and fine papillae. Scale edges darker down the middle.
Pale tan with faint body bars. Dark stripe on snout. Row of irregular white blotches from behind eye to a clear tail.
Pale body and purple rhinphores. Large translucent cerata have brownish to bright purple tips and thin white lines.
Highly ridged dark red shell with yellow and black bands. Black mantle and proboscis. White-banded tentacles.
Large red areas on dorsum extend down to become lines on a white mantle edge. Pale blue spots circled in red.
Orange dorsum covered with distinctive sharply pointed dark papillae. Pale brown gills with six branching leaves.
Pale body with green and red highlights. Club-shaped claws. Green and red antennal scales with a black spot.
Only adult males will have a large tail that extends well beyond the shell. Claws on flippers are long and curved.
Thick brown shell with two distinctively large spikes on the top. Yellow inside shell. Front aperture lip curves up.
Smooth uniformly white carapace. Fringe of short bristles on legs. Commensal with Red Heart Urchins (p. 151).
Elongate brownish-red body with white speckles. Dark edges on parapodia and rhinophore and tentacle tips.

Rows of tiny brown tentacles poking out from crevices in the reef. Pale rounded tips are sometimes striped. Flattened body column with noticeably smaller vesicles than B. antilliensis. Tentacles rarely extended in daytime.
This is a solitary (Ref. 2377) and epibenthic species found on rocky bottoms and coral reefs particularly in subtidal areas (Ref. 83915).
Salmon to dull orange encrusting sponge with distinctively protruding star-shaped veins. Large excurrent openings.
Light brown body and tapering tail. Elongated dark blotch below dorsal fin, outlined in green. Found in silty areas.
Body has numerous black and white longitudinal stripes. The shortened dorsal fin loses its white leading edge.
Found attached to hermit crab shells. Large pedal disc, variable in color from brown to reddish. Pale tentacles.
Clear bell, taller than it is wide. Four distinct orange radial canals. Up to 100 short bright orange tentacles.
Pale yellow carapace, legs and claws covered in round orange nodules. Fine yellow bristles on legs and claws.
Dark forehead and stripe on upper body. Three long thin dorsal spines. Yellow pectoral fins. Black bar on tail.
distinctive_features: Body shape: short and / or deep

description: Deep orange to yellow with small black spots. Horns not present in smallest individuals and develop as they grow.
Twin crowns of thin black spikes. Found in short tubes constructed of sand. Retracts quickly when approached.
Greenish-yellow with faint striations visible inside body. Found in loose clusters under ledges and overhangs.
A sedentary limpet-like gastropod found on coral rubble. Calcified plates with radial ridges, often overlapping.
White to greenish body with brilliant red and pale orange spots. Long tapering rhinophores, usually held forward.
Silvery body with large eyes and yellow tail. Line of dark scutes to tail base. Usually a dark patch on lower pectoral.
Bluish-green upper body, silvery below. Dark lateral keel at tail base. Lower tail lobe is longer than upper lobe.
Bluish-gray to lavender with electric blue stripe on head. Rounded tail, not pointed. No black margin on dorsal fin.
Light brown to yellow tentacles on a darker oral disc. Found encrusting the Feather Bush Hydroid (p. 136).
Maximum depth from Ref. 116013. Common but inconspicuous on reefs throughout the Caribbean (Ref. 415).
Heavily speckled greenish-gray body. Distinctive dark-rimmed, green to bluish square spot on upper rear body.
Slender mottled brown to reddish body. Tapers more towards the mouth. Rows of cone-shaped papillae.
Pale blue body with dark blue bars. Blue bar under eye and across gill cover. Pale dorsal and anal fins and tail.
distinctive_features: Body shape: short and / or deep

description: Flattened brown to reddish hemispherical body with a raised pentagonal design on the back. Short spines.
Gray to greenish-brown. Dark diamond-shaped blotches with pale centers. Row of pale spots along upper body.
Mottled brown with three dark saddles. One row of dark blotches on lower flank. Dark blotch on lower tail base.
Elongated dorsal and pelvic fins. Often yellowish color. Pelvic fin usually held downward. Small line down snout.
Transparent dome. Numerous tentacles attach to the bell and also appear to continue onto it in radial lines.
Pale body with red lines between each segment. Green spines. Short podia ending in clear or small purple balls.
Light brown to reddish body with white blotches or faint lines. Distinct lobes on head. Blue spots on parapodia.
Tall bunches of calcified and segmented stalks growing upwards from a common base. Found in the shallows.
Silvery with bluish scales and brassy cheeks. Blue line under eye. Distinctive orange blotch at corner of mouth.
distinctive_features: Body shape: short and / or deep

description: Bright orange to brownish body, paler below. Upper jaw extends beyond lower jaw. Found in shallow sand/rubble.
Three spiral rows of triangular knobs. Bands on aperture lip. Juveniles may have wavy lines similar to C. flammea.
Silvery with iridescent blue sheen. Distinctive lateral line drops sharply down below dorsal fin. Deeply forked tail.
Inhabits vertical wall faces on reef edges in areas of strong currents at depths 30 to 100 m (Ref. 415).
Translucent body with green digestive tracts. Cerata on either side of body, leaving the white pericardium visible.
Yellow spots on snout. Dark spot on upper pectoral base. Distinctively humped forehead. Florida/Gulf of Mexico.
distinctive_features: Body shape: short and / or deep

description: Stiff tubular body with a rough surface. Mouth usually bent inwards. Grows out of holes in the reef. Nocturnal.
Numerous long transparent tentacles from a hidden oral disc. Small densely packed knobs of stinging cells.
Zooxanthellate (Ref. 116012).
Maximum depth from Ref. 116012. Zooxanthellate (Ref. 116012). Occurs in most reef environments (Ref. 848).
Creamy-brown dorsum with brownish spots. Two distinct rows of knobby tubercles run from rhinophores to gills.
Pale oblong shell with dark mottling. Knobby texture on white-spotted mantle. Dark banding on pale tentacles.
Radial ribs of both valves covered with short knobby projections with white tips. Short translucent tentacles.
Zooxanthellate (Ref. 116012).
Cone-shaped shell with a short pointed spire. Deep red, often a lighter band towards the middle. Dark red mantle.
Translucent with irregular small brown spots. Distinctive straight line of several tiny black spots along upper iris.
Cream to reddish-brown disc. Red-banded arms with tiny spines. Found only on Rose Lace Coral (p. 73).
Maximum depth from Ref. 116012. Zooxanthellate (Ref. 116012).
Females with less conspicuous dorsal fin, yellow at front and black behind. Yellow to brown bars on lower cheek.
Silvery body with several yellow stripes. Often a single dark spot under soft dorsal fin (may lighten or disappear).
Head and upper front of body mottled brown. Dark and orange bars on pale abdomen. Dark spots on tail base.
Maximum depth from Ref. 116012. Zooxanthellate (Ref. 116012). Occurs in most reef environments (Ref. 848). Also in submarine caves (Ref. 87209).
Long chains of rectangular dark green segments, joined down the middle by a single stalk. Found in dense bushes.
Pale brown to whitish body covered with scattered brown spots and blotches. Two long pointed nuchal tentacles.
A thin layer of white to purplish encrusting alga. Grows on hard surfaces like shells. Also on Sea Pearls (p. 18).

At Bocas, common both in the reef and in the mangroves (Ref. 415). Commensal with amphipods (Ref. 82302, 86735). Inhabits coralline algae reef, algal nodules, and lower mesophotic reefs (Ref. 128739).
Fan-shaped blades in shades of green to brown, with pale green bands. Grows in bushes from a central stalk.
Small individuals may show clusters of small white spots on dorsum and white bars or triangles on mantle edges.
Elongated silvery body with a low concave forehead. Clear to dark yellow tail. Most often in sandy shallows.
Dark reddish body with two tapering tails of equal length. Orange and blue stripes. A solid white bar at midbody.
Orange-yellow with a thin yellow stripe. Thin blue line on head. Rare, found among deep Masked/Glass Gobies.
Yellowish to brown or gray with pale abdomen. Blunt rounded snout. Second dorsal fin almost as large as first.
Dark purple body with orange spots outlined in black. Margin and pseudotentacles lined with small white spots.
Translucent with rows of oval brown spots. Two large brown patches on abdomen outlined only in white.
Brown body with three or four wide diagonal darker bars. Body and fins have reddish spots. Florida/Gulf of Mexico.
Zooxanthellate (Ref. 116012). Found on shallow reef (Ref. 847).
Fleshy convoluted parapodia resemble leaves of lettuce. Large pale blotches on sides. Long curled rhinophores.
Flattened gray to purple dorsum with dark blotches and raised central area. Brown rhinophores with white tips.
Green with distinctive longitudinal white stripes. Solid black line along parapodial edge. Yellow-tipped rhinophores.
White to reddish-brown body with distinctive thin dark lines and a white margin. Close-set pseudotentacles.
Light yellow to black blotched body with thin irregular stripes. Thin white lines radiate from eye, over gill cover.
Tan to gray with tufts of cirri and dark spots in faint lines. Small pectoral fin and fleshy groove at top of gill cover.
distinctive_features: Body shape: short and / or deep

description: Thin white bar on black snout. Thin white or blue stripe from top of eye to tail. On sponges. Belize to Honduras.
Orange to brown shell with ridges and a long curving aperture lip. Pale mantle with dark reddish blotches.
Thick rough-sculptured shell. Tall major ribs with a wavy pattern. Knobby white mantle with distinct dark bands.
Rounded silvery body with narrow tail base. Oblique black stripes on upper back. Dark spots behind pectoral.
Elongated semi-transparent body with a single silver stripe. Found in shallow waters, singly or small schools.
First dorsal fin tall, often white. Last bar narrow, indented at top and not darker. Prominent orange midbody scales.

Common on hard, inshore, shallow bottoms and on seagrass beds; less common on shallow reefs. Internal canal system supports an abundance and diverse invertebrate fauna. Known from depths between 0.5 to about 18 m (Ref. 85482).
Distinctively short thick neck and a bulbous head. The tall rounded shell is often encrusted with barnacles.
Rounded cerata with white markings. Rhinophores with distinctive bulges. Feeds on the eggs of other sea slugs.
Elongated rounded body with short brown spines. Longer clear spines on the top and towards the rear.
A less common variation with white spines. Individuals may be found with both black and white spines together.
Overall pattern black and white. Front of dorsal fin tall, edged with white band. Often a dark circle on gill cover.
Black to dark gray. Both dorsal and anal fins extend well beyond tail base. Bright blue leading edge on anal fin.
distinctive_features: Body shape: short and / or deep

description: Hollow hood-shaped rostrum. Hooked dactyls. Last legs 1.5x longer than carapace. Rubble and seagrass areas.
Elongated orange body. Two distinctive white spots on head. White tips on rhinophores and long oral tentacles.
Golden stripes on reddish body. White-tipped spines on first dorsal fin. Distinctively long white anal fin. Red tail.
Color varies, often red or black. Transparent spots on tail and fins, mimicking sponges. Large blunt first dorsal fin.
distinctive_features: Body shape: short and / or deep

description: Distinctively elongated snout. Orange bar above eye. Dorsal fins are darker, pelvic and anal fins are yellow.
distinctive_features: Body shape: short and / or deep

description: Often in dark colors and found in shallow seagrass beds, they are released from the brood pouch fully formed.
Pointed rostrum. Sides of carapace deeply bilobed. Long claw arms. Distinctive triangular white mask on the back.
Distinctively hairy carapace and legs. Deep red claw arms with numerous short spines. Black tips on claws.
Bright white triangular blotches behind tips of dorsal spines. Elongated rear dorsal fin. Long tail base, long upper tail lobe.
Round flattened shell with curved triangular spines arranged spirally from the center. Prefers shallows.
Transparent body with a rounded hump towards a long transparent tail. A second set of claws behind the first.
Narrow silvery body with bluish tinge. Very steep head profile. Long dorsal and anal fins. Prefers shallow areas.
Maximum depth from Ref. 83917. Zooxanthellate (Ref. 116012).
Thin-walled encrusting sponge with low star-shaped veins around excurrent openings. Color highly variable.

Known from rocky bottoms (Ref. 83912). Widely distributed in shallow reef habitats, lagoons (Ref. 415), mangrove and seagrass. Early colonizer of shallow artificial reefs (Ref. 85482). Commensal with amphipods (Ref. 82302).
Distinctive blue line runs along dorsum, divides on the head and continues onto long white-tipped oral tentacles.
Bluish-green upper body. Often a yellow midbody stripe. No spots on lateral line. Dark spot on upper gill cover.
Blue to gold rings and spots. Midbody blotch. Convex (not notched) forehead. Males have long pectoral spines.
distinctive_features: Body shape: short and / or deep

description: The largest of the feather dusters, with a double row of banded radioles. Variable in color: tan, yellow or purple.
Dark purple body with radiating red lines. Long spines are clustered in radial bands down the body segments.
Cream to reddish-brown valves. Distinct radial ribs (with fine concentric threads) lead to serrated valve margins.
Robust thick-lipped black and white shell. Found near rocky inshores. A commercially harvested species.
Wide dark brown bars. Thin white lines in interspaces. Red dorsal and anal fins. Dark spot at pectoral base.
