Pale body with a single dark midbody spot. Stripe from snout to top of gill cover. Pelvic and anal fins are pale.
Thin tubular green leaves growing in clumps that can cover a wide area. Often found among Turtle Grass.
Translucent orange body and siphons. Grows clustered in sandy reef areas, mangroves and seagrass beds.
Long flattened green to bluish appendages scattered within the tentacles. Mostly in lagoons and mangroves.
Translucent body with highly variable green, brown and white highlights. Banded legs. Often near anemones.
Numerous thin radial ribs, often covered with commensal sponge, as seen above. Short red and white tentacles.
Light blue to pale purple body. Dark borders on blue dorsal and anal fins. Small dark pectorals. Nocturnal.
Very tall first dorsal fin. Two ocelli on rear dorsal fin, one on rear anal fin. Branched single stalk cirrus over eye.
Rounded bell, often with marbled black markings. Bulbous oral arms. Shorter clusters of white tentacles.
Gray to light tan body with diffuse dark blotches behind pectoral fin. Two distinctive skin flaps on upper back.
distinctive_features: Body shape: short and / or deep

description: Green body with a distinctive cleft on the right side behind the head. Found on Halimeda algae (p. 22).
Tentacles and body are deep purple to maroon. Often found encrusting on Dark Volcano Sponges (p. 51).
Mottled red and orange encrusting alga. Forms a rough fan shape with darker lines radiating from the center.
The most common goby. Similar to Glass Goby but no dark scale edges or spots. White dash on pectoral fin.
Dark bar below eye. Dark spot or saddle at tail base. Blue pelvic and dark pectoral fins. Dark borders on tail.
distinctive_features: Body shape: short and / or deep

description: Indistinct dark bars not extending over dorsal fin. Ocellus as in L. cricota (compare to white ring in L. nuchipinnis).
Zooxanthellate (Ref. 116012). Common throughout the Caribbean on protected shallow reefs or deeper reefs (Ref. 415).
This overgrowing tunicate is most often a dull gray color but can be found in white, bluish-green or dark purple.
Color and density are highly variable, in loose patches of individuals or in clusters. May show bright fluorescence.
Reddish head. Last body bar is black, narrower than the white bar in front of it. Red tail with single thin white bar.
Gray with white on dorsum. Pink cerata with white bands, widest past mid-point. Red stripe on lower rhinophores.
Dark body with yellowish area on back of the head and rhinophores. Blue and orange half-moons on the sides.
Maximum depth from Ref. 116012. Zooxanthellate (Ref. 116012).
Oval shell has white spots with brown centers (banded in juveniles). Mantle color variable, gray to brown or black.
Reddish body with numerous long banded tentacles. Sharp bristles along the body used to move inside a sandy tube.
Bulbous bright yellow to orange sponge. Surface forms lumpy melted blobs around small excurrent openings.
Broad fan-shaped green blades on a single short stalk. Distinctive lines radiate to the edge of the blades.
Thin ragged-edged cup of light green, attached by a thin stalk. Often found in sandy areas in large groupings.
Yellow bar on yellow snout. Yellow stripe fades to white, from top of eye to tail. On sponges. Mexico to Honduras.
Dark blue body and fins. Paler blue facial markings. Large beak often covered in algae. Pointed lobes on tail.
Short dark green leaves with an elongated oval shape, growing in pairs. Grows on a short stalk on runners.
Thick-walled pale shell with a creamy color inside. Body whorl with large rounded spikes. On sand and seagrass.
Dark bars extend onto fins. Front of dorsal fin is short. Prominent ocellus on gill cover with orange posterior rim.
Sometimes dark with scattered white spots concentrated around rhinophores. Green color varies as they feed.
Bar behind dorsal fin narrows to point above abdomen (compare: A. robinsi). Often a darker bar at tail base.
distinctive_features: Body shape: short and / or deep

description: Only found on shallow corals or encrusting fire corals. Colors vary, often combinations of pastels and black.
White shell with reddish-brown wavy lines. Pale mantle with large white spots and greenish-yellow margins.
Iridescent dark blue body above and silvery below. Dark pectoral fin with broad clear margin and short clear bar.
Prominent dark bars extend to edge of dorsal fin. Ocellus on gill cover is indistinct, with a smudgy orange outline.
Olive-green with dark bars. Pearly spots on upper back and head. Distinctive mohawk of red and white filaments.
Light brown segments often with dark edges. Distinctive rounded W marking on tail. Usually attaches near gills.
Flattened rounded encrusting alga. Orange-brown to rusty base color. Concentric pattern of dark red lines.
Individual tunicates cluster around the excurrent siphons, giving them a mottled appearance. Color varies greatly.
Head densely covered in small pores. Dark branched nasal cirrus. 2nd spot on dorsal fin dark, touching base.
Slender silvery body (may show faint bars). Concave below lower jaw. Large eye with dark spot above iris.
Five tubular arms with rounded tips. Red with irregular dark red mottling. Rows of small pits run down the arms.
Translucent yellowish-brown or green to black. Irregular brown mottling and white patches. An active swimmer.
Flattened green carapace with dark brown lines. Yellow spots on claw arms. Pink claws. Found in the shallows.
Brownish tunicates growing in clusters from a common tunic. Outer edges of the siphons are pale and mottled.
Conical red to purplish shell with a pointed spire. Finely beaded spiral ribs. Dark mantle with small white spots.
Zooxanthellate (Ref. 116012).
Conical spire. Bluish-gray with brown axial streaks. Top of final whorl has low white knobs. Dark red proboscis.
Yellowish-brown body with blue stripes. Blue on top lip and iris. Distinctive thin blue line from cheek to gill cover.
Tangled mass of soft thick branching stalks. White to light brown. Slimy appearance. Can cover large areas.
Green calcified stalks with darker tips. Grows in clusters, but not fused. Usually only found in very shallow water.
Plates colors are highly variable, including green, purple, orange or pale pink. Girdle often with faint dark bands
If the animal has not been feeding, the cerata’s digestive tracts are empty and it appears pale or even transparent.
Tiny upside-down mushroom shapes over top of eye. Mottled red to brownish body. Two indistinct bars on tail.
Pale brown thick-walled shell circled with distinctive thin brown lines. White mantle with brown and gold markings.
Zooxanthellate (Ref. 116012). Reef flat (Ref. 811). In Belize, it was found in a submarine cave near Columbus Cay at a depth of 10 m (Ref. 87209).
Mottled reddish-brown body, sometimes with faint bands. Dark brown and pale spots. Red eye set close to snout.
Distinctive small black spot on upper flank. Faint blue line under eye. Reddish tail and pectoral fin. Red iris.
Clusters of thin flat leaves becoming narrower with each branching. Olive-brown with a bright green iridescence.
Five dark brown to red body bars. Distinctive large black saddle on upper tail base. Gold-tipped dorsal spines.
Translucent white dorsum with small white papillae and pale blotches. Rolled rhinophores and a wide oral veil.

Inhabits coral heads. Removes ectoparasites on skin and fins, in the mouth and in the gill chambers of greater fish like groupers and others (Ref. 92840). Monogamous (Ref. 52884). Minimum depth reported from Ref. 27115. Has been reared in captivity (Ref. 35420).
Pale translucent body with white-edged body segments and scattered black spots. Long white lines on head.
Tufts of bristle-like light green branchlets growing from a common stalk. White skeletal material on the surface.
Smooth creamy-white shell with a pointed spire. Dark brown wavy interconnecting lines surround the shell.
White body with a dense reticulated pattern of brown markings. Black lines on the head and around margin.
Transparent body with two white bands across the back to the sides. Small white eyes. Found on gorgonians.
Brownish body with five dark body bars. Long upper lip. Rounded tail lobes. Found on shallow rocky inshores.
distinctive_features: Body shape: short and / or deep

description: Flattened carapace, circled by a distinctive thin green line. Yellow bands and red spots on legs. Red eyes.
Black body with nine narrow bright blue bars. Red to pale brown head and red Iris. Found in clear shallow waters.
Gray to orange with white spots. Bulbous cerata with white nipples. Pale oral tentacles with thin orange bands.
Postero-lateral spine broad, continuous with body. Short horns on rostrum (compare to long on M. trispinosum).
Deep grooves and knobs on a mottled green to reddish carapace. Three blunt spines to the rear. Banded legs.
Dome-shaped carapace with nodules in contrasting colors. Short bristles on claw arms. Found in rubble.
Variable black and white markings, often with pale blue near the head and tail. No orange lines on parapodia.
Twin-lobed pale brown blades growing in small clusters. The tips of each blade are edged in a lighter brown.
Pale brown with distinctive loose folds of skin. Around fifteen dark narrow vertical crossbars and more on tail.
distinctive_features: Body shape: short and / or deep

description: Gray with black and white speckles. Dorsal fins set close to long tail. Fleshy barbels over top lip. Small white eye.
Chalk-white with red spots on cerata tips. Rhinophores have distinctive swellings and small reddish striations.
Translucent to whitish body with white internal structures visible. White lines around siphons. Found in clusters.
Uniformly blue or greenish body with no bars. Blue lines radiate behind eye. Always yellow-orange pectoral fin.
distinctive_features: Body shape: short and / or deep

description: Uniformly gray body. Black spot at pectoral base. Small black eyes. Short spike under abdomen, before anal fin.
distinctive_features: Body shape: short and / or deep

description: Rounded dorsal fin with a diffuse white tip. Pectoral fins are very long and also rounded with white tips. Pelagic.
Pale blotches with yellow speckles, formed by a network of dark lines on top of carapace. Large claws cover the face.
Reddish-brown carapace with two distinctive large dark spots to the rear, each circled in white. Darker claw tips.
Three large dark ocellated spots: on lower dorsal fin, on midbody and on tail. Elongated filaments on lure.
distinctive_features: Body shape: short and / or deep

description: White to pale brownish body with scattered dots. Two distinctive black spots near tail base, circled in white.
distinctive_features: Body shape: short and / or deep

description: Unmarked mantle is uniform in color. Can be purple, pale pink or white, depending on the host gorgonian.
Orange body and pale brown to yellow oral disc. Long transparent tentacles tipped with bright orange balls.
Occurs in medium-energy reef environments and deep hard bottoms (Ref. 85482).
Azooxanthellate (Ref. 116012). Can be found in shallow waters (Ref. 122680). Found on rock overhangs (Ref. 88997). In Hong Kong, also found in marine caves (Ref. 120542).

Maximum thickness: 10 cm (Ref. 415). Maximum depth reported taken from Ref. 128739. Found in coral reefs, usually below 10 m in depth (Ref. 415), and on rocky bottoms (Ref. 83912). It hosts alpheid shrimps, i.e., Synalpheus spp. in its canals (Ref. 86672). Inhabits shallow and mesophotic coral reefs along with coral communities and coralline algae reefs (Ref. 128739).
Narrow flattened body with dense tufts of white bristles. Orange, red or greenish, often darker towards midbody.

It is an epibiotic species found on hard substrates (Ref. 108813). In reefs, encrusting the undersides of corals. Occasionally in mangroves where it can grow to record size (Ref. 415). Lines the edges of plate-like or overhanging stony coral colonies (Ref. 85482).

Maximum thickness: 2 cm (Ref. 415). Maximum depth reported taken from Ref. 128739. It is common on mangrove roots; peat banks; and on shallow reefs habitats (Ref. 415). Inhabits coralline algae reefs, algal nodules, shallow coral reefs and mangroves (Ref. 128739).
Orange teardrop-shaped shell with narrow white bands. Mantle with white blotches and dense orange spots.
Oblong shell with dark mottling and small orange spots. Orange spots and white blotches on mantle and proboscis.
Green to translucent body with white patches and orange spots. White stripes on separated clusters of cerata.
Upper head and body dark orange. Two orange squares just behind eye, circled in black. Orange bar on snout.
Elongated, with rounded fins and long hunting tentacles. Photophore patch on dorsal mantle near head. Pelagic.
Small individuals are often darker brown, with radiating white spots on gill and rhinophore sheaths more visible.
The left claw is larger, with pale yellow tips. Orange antennae and eyestalks. Claws are sometimes pale blue.
Translucent with two red patches on abdomen, outlined in black and then white. Similar dashes along spinal column.
Orange spots on head and front of body. Rear of body with distinctive black and white bars. Found in shallows.
Dark brown with pale stripes and small orange spots on body. Orange lines on head. Shy, found close to the reef.
distinctive_features: Body shape: short and / or deep

description: Pairs of dark blotches down side, often joining to form an H shape. Dark bands on tail. Elongated first dorsal spine.
Short, almost triangular rostrum. Commonly covered in filamentous red algae. Found in reef and rubble areas.
Black with distinctive white patches: near eyes, under chin and on abdomen towards tail. Rarely seen, pelagic.
Plates are patterned in green, brown or red. Deep red girdle sometimes showing pale spots or thin bands.
Light green body with tiny black and white spots. Tips of rhinophores and parapodia lined with orange and black.
White valves with dense radial ribs and broken pattern of reddish-brown bands. Found among shallow rubble.
Orange to brown shell with a white spire tip and aperture. Creamy white markings form into bands. Purplish mantle.
Large head and tapering body, both with small orange spots. Branching cirrus over eye. Found in shallows.
Spongy paddle-shaped dark green blade with a distinctively thickened stalk. Prefers clear shallow water.
15 white bands across a dark body, with orange spots where they meet tufts of bristles from each segment.
Prominent curling rhinophores tipped in red. Parapodia lined with red, blue and green. Yellow Y on the head.
Transparent body with purple circles “painted” around the siphons. Often found in dense clusters of individuals.
White to creamy-yellow oval dorsum with distinctive rows of yellow glands. Rhinophores pale to golden brown.
Pale pink head and body with pearly highlights. Thin dark bar behind second dorsal fin and a wider bar at tail base.
Blunt snout. Head is often reddish. Bars do not extend onto the dorsal fin membranes. Second bar wide at top.
Pale body with yellow to orange markings. Band from eye to jaw. Black blotches on iris. Dark bar at tail base.
Distinctively elongated black dorsal and anal fins. Four thin body bars. Often found schooling in the shallows.
distinctive_features: Body shape: short and / or deep

description: Palp worms bury into sand, extending two tentacles to feed. This species is recognized by its colored bands.
Rounded teardrop-shaped body with spots and bands. Tapering tail. Fast-moving predators, hence their name.
Pointed rostrum has a distinctively white tip. Dark patch around eye. Larger or coastal individuals develop spots.
Reddish-orange head and front body. Rear body with four reddish-brown bars and spots. May show blue spots.
Tan body with white and brown spots. Cirrus branches out in a single plane. Blunt papillae on head and snout.
Pale green body with white papillae on parapodial edge. Distinctive brown band on lower third of the rhinophores.
Thin rounded green blades with lighter veins running towards the edges in a distinctively star-shaped pattern.
Pale with brown and white spots. White bridle under eye. Bar (not spots) at tail base. No spot on lower pectoral fin.
Yellowish-green body with white papillae. Three flaps on dark-rimmed parapodia, the middle one distinctively long.
Flattened blades of purplish or red to pink alga with a distinctive network of holes and frills on the outer edges.
Blue rosettes on body and blue spots on head and fins. Dark bars on pectoral fin in larger fish. Notched forehead.
distinctive_features: Body shape: short and / or deep

description: Greenish with dark bars. Pearly-white spots on upper body, dark spots on nape. Prominent dark bars on lips.
Long thin translucent body, tapering to a point. Lives inside the anal cavity of some sea cucumber species.
Bright orange zooids form a calcified layer. Often found on mangrove roots and dock pilings as well as on reefs.
The largest of the razorfishes. Blue lines under a small eye. Blunt forehead. Often a single diffuse body bar.
Dark purple spots on claw arms, back and tail. Long thin antennae. Found on Corkscrew Anemones (p. 121).
Striped body. Dark spots on both rear dorsal and anal fins. Spots on tail are joined. All spots edged in white.
Translucent yellow body. Blue on top of snout and upper edge of eye. Three blue lines from eye, upper one short.
Tall silvery body, often with yellow blotch on abdomen and dusky oval patch behind dark pectoral. Dark anal fin.
distinctive_features: Body shape: short and / or deep

description: Translucent gray to light brown with white and brown spots. Rhinophores distinctively tall with conical tips.
Zooxanthellate (Ref. 116012).
Globular yellowish to translucent gray body covered with short unbranched papillae. Internal organs are visible.
Thin plate-like leaves growing upwards in a concentric pattern and forming a ball shape. Often pointed at the tip.
Bluish-silver with blue and yellow stripes. Dark spot on top of lateral line. Yellow anal fin with bright blue edge.
Pink encrusting sponge with red speckles. Excurrent openings are a lighter pink and slightly protruding.
Clusters of long pink stalks growing upwards from a common base. Short pink branchlets tipped in white.
Tall bushy pink alga with long thin central branches. Smaller light pink branchlets grow out in a single plane.
Smooth flattened bell, pale pink to purplish or orange. Four large tufted lobes and thin tentacles over 20 m long.
Small tangled clumps of very fine calcified branching stalks with pale tips. Shades of pink with white joints.

On coral reefs and hard bottoms (Ref. 85482), and in mangroves (Ref. 86836). Commensal with amphipods (Ref. 86735). In Belize, it was found in a submarine cave near Columbus Cay at a depth of 10 m (Ref. 87209).
Smaller individuals are usually darker, with fewer fleshy appendages and often hide in lagoons and seagrass.
Maximum depth reported taken from Ref. 128739. Common on open reef habitats more than 3 m deep (Ref. 415), and in mangroves (Ref. 86836). Inhabits heavily silted lower mesophotic reefs (Ref. 128739).
A pelagic nudibranch species that is rarely seen drifting over a reef. Transparent body circled with white spots.
Transparent body, often with blue markings. Faint muscles and reddish digestive tracts can usually be seen. Pelagic.
Maroon body with a large right claw with white spots. Bright orange legs. Blue eyes on banded eyestalks.
Pale brown with yellowish-orange patches behind eye. Dark spots on pale pectorals. Florida/Gulf of Mexico.
distinctive_features: Body shape: short and / or deep

description: Light gray with a paler abdomen. Small black spots on body and fins. No dark bars (compare: C. reticulatus).
distinctive_features: Body shape: short and / or deep

description: Two wide black bars on head, one through eye and another behind gill cover to below pectoral fin. All fins are yellow.
distinctive_features: Body shape: short and / or deep

description: Pale purple gas-filled float with pinched edge along top. Purple trailing tentacles can reach up to 50 m / 160 ft.
Tan body tapering at the rear, mottled with rows of brown blotches. Podia of various sizes, tipped with tiny papillae.
Pale body with three dark stripes from snout to tail base. Diffuse dark borders on tail (compare: S. iseri juvenile).
Orange-brown body, often white blotches on upper back from nape to tail base. Blue lines from eye and on cheek.
Dark bars extend slightly onto dorsal fin. White patch on front of dorsal fin. Orangish lower head with white spots.
Pale white to tan eel-like body, the upper part peppered with dark dots. Dorsal and anal fins not connected to tail.
Dense clusters of fleshy-textured deep purple branches. Branchlets slightly flattened and tapered at the tips.
Zooxanthellate (Ref. 116012). In Belize, it was found in a submarine cave near Columbus Cay at a depth of 10 m (Ref. 87209).
Small domed circular to oval colonies, depressed in the center. Pale to deep bluish purple zooids in single rows.
Purple fan of widely spaced stalks, rarely branching and without red tips. Shallows to deeper sheltered reefs.
Dark blue to bluish-gray body. Bright blue spots on head and blue crescent above eye. Found on deeper reefs.
distinctive_features: Body shape: short and / or deep

description: White dorsum with large black blotches. Broad yellow band around mantle edge. Purple gills and rhinophores.
Brownish body with small purple spots on dorsum and sides. White gills lined in brown. White rhinophores.
Colors variable from brown to purplish. Dorsal fin black with thin white margin. Yellow iris. Dark inside mouth.
Translucent to orange body. Long distinctively pointed cerata and rhinophores, with purple rings and pale tips.
Color varies from gray to dark brown. Distinctive pointed lobe at midbody. Two equal-sized rounded lobes on tail.
Pale tan body with dark longitudinal lines of broken spots and dashes. Often found hiding in sargassum (p. 24).
distinctive_features: Body shape: short and / or deep

description: Uniform reddish to orange with faint bars alternating with thin rows of white spots. Internal white band at midbody.
Highly variable in apex height and form. Concentric threads cross the ribs, giving a latticelike appearance.
Blue with yellow scale edges. Spot on nape, circled in pale blue. Blue on lips and edge of gill cover. Bright yellow tail.
distinctive_features: Body shape: short and / or deep
abundance: Common in Florida, Bahamas, Gulf of Mexico, Bermuda.
habitat_behavior: Swim slowly in the vicinity of sea whips, sea fans, and corals.
diver_reaction: Wary, tend to keep their distance.

description: Orange to brown shell is pink inside. The proboscis and eyestalks are mottled. Small eyes with black tentacles.
Brownish-gray upper body with diffuse white stripe from pectoral to lower tail base. No markings on a gray face.
Yellowish-gray body with thin dark diagonal stripes and small spots. Usually found close to shelter on the reef.
distinctive_features: Body shape: short and / or deep

description: Greatly elongated first dorsal fin, extended into filaments in males. Large white-edged black ocellus on gill cover.
Thin light brown valves with radiating white lines and visible concentric rings. May grow in dense clusters.
Flat olive-green leaves growing upwards from a central stalk. Leaves are not as dense and end in a blunt top.
The largest of the parrotfishes. Orange-bronze head and green body. Large scales. Orange tail with pointed lobes.
Silvery to olive upper body and yellow tail. Two blue stripes, with yellow in between, from eye to tail base.
Blue stripes on snout and above eye. Reddish dorsal fin. Dark blotch at tail base, orange stripe onto center of tail.
Highly variable in color and markings. All have a small opening in the parapodia, at middle-rear of the dorsum.
The dark area in the center of the body may be speckled with white spots. The mantle margin remains orange.
Pink to tan body with serrated head and purplish-red spots. Transparent sides. Found on Swimming Crinoids (p. 147).
Carapace with orange patches outlined in red. Red legs and claw arms banded with white. Prefers deeper reefs.
Bushy, with slightly flattened red branches. Branchlets are short with darker tips. Prefers areas of strong surge.
Reddish-purple body with darker red cross-bars. May be solid red or blue-spotted. Shallow, often under urchins.
Flattened red carapace with white markings. Red and white bands on legs. Smooth red claws with white tips.
Thick round blades, often fused and overlapping. Red to maroon, with brownish edges. Found in dark reef areas.
Thin-walled encrusting sponge in shades of pink to red. Large excurrent openings with dark red radiating veins.
Tangled masses of fine hair-like deep red filaments, each repeatedly branching outwards. May cover large areas.
Orange-brown girdle almost covers the entire body. Tufts of short bristles extend from sides of each plate.
Reddish-brown body with scattered white spots. White blotches behind eye and sometimes on snout. Dark tail.
Rounded dome-shaped body, flattened on the bottom. Short brown spines. Buries into sand during the day.
Large red spots over a lighter body. Dorsal, pelvic and anal fins and tail all have black margins edged in white.
Red bars on head and body. Large pectoral arrays. Long dorsal and anal spines. Fleshy appendages around mouth.
Brown to reddish body with small blue markings. Broken white line down side. Small dark spot just behind snout.
Five broad arms tipped with black. Sometimes white markings on the top. Cryptic, found hiding in rubble.
The carapace, legs and claws are a uniform bright red. Indentations on claws. Green eyes on yellow eyestalks.
Red body with white spots on abdomen and dark spots above. Green-gold spots on claws and down the back.
Reddish pedal disc covered with pale warty knobs. Short pointed olive-green tentacles with red and yellow bands.
The claws are of equal size with red bands and scattered small white spots. Gold antennae and eyestalks. Blue eyes.
Orange body with red markings at the joints. Large claws of different sizes. Shy, found hiding in holes in the sand.
Carapace with long grooved indentations. Two sharp spines found just inside the claw arms. Smooth claws.
Translucent white dorsum with red speckles. Covered in low rounded tubercles. White gills and rhinophores.
Legs and claws are orange to cream with thin dark red stripes. The right claw is much larger. Bluish eyes.
Short bush of cylindrical pale pink branches. Alternating branchlets with distinctively red tips and fine filaments.
White dorsum with ruffled mantle, outlined in red and bright yellow. Red tips on white gills and rhinophores.
Solid greenish-blue body. All fins are pink to red, except a clear pectoral. Often a white spot at upper tail base.
Bright red rear body and anal fin. Final body bar black and oval. Base of tail with red bar and then white bar.
Pale head with red bands. Over 20 thin bars. Honduras/ Belize: T. multifasciatus elsewhere, without cheek bands.
Iridescent bluish-green with faint dark stripes and four orange stripes. Distinctive orange spot above gill cover.
Dome-shaped bristled carapace with a distinctive dark blotch in the middle. Robust claws are tipped in red.
Distinctive reddish to orange highlights on dorsal and anal fins and lobes of tail. Long dark bar on gill cover.
Red to yellowish-brown carapace. Long tufts of red hair along the tops of claw arms. Pale bands on the claws.
Blue dorsum with dark blue rhinophores and gills. Mantle edged in yellow with a thin red margin. Prefers shallows.
Blunt snout. Red lower lip and pectoral fin. Wide mouth with comb-like teeth. Perches on shallow coral heads.
Elongated body with short oral lobes. Cilia appear as lines of flashing red spots running parallel to the gut.
Tall barred body. Bright red spots on face and dorsal fin. Red tassels on dorsal spine tips. Dark bar at tail base.
Bluish-green body, pale abdomen. Dark spot at base of pectoral, large blue blotch behind it. Red crescent on tail.
Slender silvery body with a bluish stripe. Red dorsal and pectoral fins and tail. Pelagic, may school on deep reefs.
Reddish-brown body with large dark patch on gill cover. Dark tail, rear dorsal and anal fin. Shy, hides in crevices.
Dark yellow body above and almost white below. Wide dark bar at tail base, running onto dorsal and anal fins.
distinctive_features: Body shape: short and / or deep

description: A broad calcified crust of pink to gray alga that takes on the shape of the underlying reef, holding it in place.
Gray to coppery body with dark spot at pectoral base. Large reddish eye. Closely related to the drums (p. 348).
Brick red to mottled dark brown with distinctively dark, almost black, eyes. Darker red banding on claw arms.
Dark brown to red body. Yellow spots on large pectoral fins and tail. Small black spot on rear of first dorsal fin.
Red body with thin white stripes. Dorsal fin red with white tips. Large black blotch across first three dorsal spines.
Smooth rounded body with large incurrent and excurrent siphons. Color variable from brown to lavender or purple.
Dark red body. Short pointed spines all have darker tips and a pale ring around their bases. Prefers shallow reefs.
Tapering brown body. Black vertical fins fused with tail, paler at the base to the front. Found deep inside caves.
Rounded shell with longitudinal ridges. Broad broken lines of brown or black and white surround the shell.
Brown to light green central disc with thin dark lines in a reticulated pattern. Long arms with thin dark bands.
Light brown body with numerous spots and a darker medial line. Distinctive thin black line around the margin.
Translucent brown body. Bristled claws have reddish lines in a reticulated pattern. Found in sandy areas.
Thick-walled shells with pitted grooves. Opening covered by stony plates when not feeding. Shallow to intertidal.
Rounded white shell with a blunt spire and well spaced ribs. Mantle and proboscis are pale with white blotches.
Pointed snout. Single long tapering cirrus. Arching dorsal fin with an irregular orange blotch at base of first spines.
Bright orange body, legs and claws. The right claw is much larger. Legs and claw arms have thin white bands.
Maximum depth from Ref. 116012. Zooxanthellate (Ref. 116012). Occurs in most reef environments (Ref. 848).
Inhabits rich coral tops of drop-offs (Ref. 9710) to at least 175 m. Associated with Condylactis gigantea (Ref. 9710).
Calcified dark green blades growing in a single plane or forming into a low bush. Thicker than similar species.
Golden-yellow body with large diffuse black blotch, often containing a bright blue circle. Blue ring around eye.
distinctive_features: Body shape: short and / or deep

description: Reddish-brown spots on body and fins. Three dark saddles below dorsal fin and another above tail base.
Short brown spines with a thick base. Black or red body. Found boring holes into the limestone substrate by day.
Reddish shell with white blotches. Distinctively flared tips grow both above the eyes and at rear of aperture lip.

Maximum depth from Ref. 83917. Zooxanthellate (Ref. 116012). Small and free-living colonies in subtidal seagrass beds; attached and hemispherical in shallow reef environments (Ref. 848). Also common in sand and coral rubble (Ref. 415)
Rounded pink to red encrusting alga with faint concentric and radial lines. Margins slightly raised from the substrate.
Color varies, with dark upper body and pale lower body, with indistinct blotches and heavy dark/light speckling.
Yellow cheek and gill cover with thin blue bars. Vertical blue lines on scales. Darkened pectoral base. Red iris.
Lumpy yellowish to brown carapace, shorter than it is wide. Ridge above claws with distinctively sharp teeth.
Zooxanthellate (Ref. 116012). Occurs in shallow reef environments (Ref. 848).
Rounded valves and deep red mantle folds. Long white, red or pink tentacles. Found hiding in narrow crevices.
Narrow V-shaped notch on front of carapace. White- banded legs. Distinctive ridges on front of claw arms.
Creamy light brown scales with a distinctively rough texture of pointed tubercles. Thick tapering cirri on sides.
Maximum depth from Ref. 116012. Zooxanthellate (Ref. 116012). Occurs in most reef environments, especially in surge channels (Ref. 848).
Long narrow head with a sloping forehead and long rostrum. White patch above eye continues onto back.
Raised head with short rostrum. Conical spines on back. Small mouth with reddish lips. Color is highly variable.
distinctive_features: Body shape: short and / or deep

description: Bulbous head with upturned rostrum and often a white line below eye. Abdominal sections have pointed edges.
Females are usually yellow with dark spots on gill cover and base of pectoral fin. Eyes are always bright orange.
Often has pale blue spots. First dorsal fin usually short. Last body bar is darkest and wider than pale bar in front.
Dark bar behind second dorsal fin continues to anal fin. Dark borders on dorsal and anal fins. Wide bar at tail base.
Two distinctively long horns on rostrum, almost touching. Two slightly curved white spines at rear of carapace.
Elongated silvery body. A line of small black dots along curved part of lateral line. Large scutes near dusky tail.
Common on reefs, 2 to 20 m deep (Ref. 415).
Flattened gelatinous stalks, seldom branching, form a ball shape. Pink to reddish-brown. Prefers deeper reefs.
Low clusters of fleshy red to iridescent pink leaves. Blade margins are often branched and overlapping.
Long thin arms have short spines and are banded with red and white. Color and patterns are highly variable.
Narrow fan shape extending out from a single stalk. Thick-walled. Dark green with pale edges on the blade.
Reddish-orange encrusting sponge with dark veins and a distinctively transparent outer sheath. Found in shallows.
Translucent white body with visible internal organs on medial line. Black eyespots, set well back from margin.
Wide reddish to orange bars on head and body. Red bands on forehead. Red spots on tail and dorsal fin. Often upside down.
Saddled Blennies are highly variable in color, from tan to yellowish to red-spotted. The saddles may be indistinct.
Dark spot and orange rim on tall dorsal fin. Blue spots on head. Divot at upper pupil. Can pale or darken quickly.
Silvery-gray. Dark spots on scales form thin diagonal stripes. Tail and fins are dark, except for a pale pectoral.
distinctive_features: Body shape: short and / or deep

description: Pale green to dark brown with bright speckles on dense cerata. Brown line on rhinophores. Found on fine sand.
Brown bars join diamond-shaped blotches on sides. Final bar is an inverted Y. Dark spot at upper edge of gill cover.
Flat brown disc-shaped body with a dark pentagonal design on the back. Very short spines. Buries in sand.
Flattened white carapace with reddish-brown mottling. Bristled legs. Commensal with Sand Dollars (p. 150).
Orange-brown with pale blue stripes. Darker bars while resting. Blue markings on snout (compare: D. bivittatum).
Light rusty-brown with gray or reddish spots and short papillae. Leaf-shaped oral tentacles. May bury in sand.
Grayish body with irregular bands. Lives in a symbiotic relationship with the Orangespotted Goby (p. 294).
Yellow stripe from eye, broadening towards tail. Large black spot on tail base. Hovers close to a small burrow.
As in C. tortugae but with darker blotch on lower pectoral base, dark bar at tail base. Prefers clean, deeper water.
Narrow brown blades with serrated edges. No prominent central vein. Gas floats with distinctively pointed tips.
Tough leathery greenish-brown leaves with darker brown blotches. Gas floats connect by a flattened stalk.
Flattened body tapering to the rear, with scattered brown spots and irregular blotches. Small thin white tentacles.
Light brown with white blotches or blue spots. Tall lobes on back. Found camouflaged in sargassum rafts (p. 24).
Brownish-gold body with thin pale bars and long snout. Found hiding in sargassum (p. 24). Rarely seen on reefs.
Long serrated blades with pale central vein. Gas floats are rounded. Gathers in wide mats of floating vegetation.
White spots on dorsum may join as patches, the sides are solid brown. Curved tapering cerata with white tips.
Brown to gold with white blotches. Yellow edges on arms and claws. Found hiding in floats of sargassum (p. 24).
Purple body with rows of small black spots. Three dark lines on cheek. White patch above eye. Tail edged in red.
distinctive_features: Body shape: short and / or deep

description: Numerous fleshy tabs and small white spots. Found camouflaged in floats of Sargassum Seaweed (p. 24).
distinctive_features: Body shape: short and / or deep

description: Broad fan-shaped dark green blades with a spongy tex-ture. Lower edge turns upward to attach to the stalk.
Triangular cone-shaped thickened leaves on a short stalk. Often with dark brown speckles and pale centers.
Bluish cheek with small yellow spots. Curved blue line under eye. Blue spots on pectoral base and top of gill cover.
distinctive_features: Body shape: short and / or deep

description: Short narrow bluish-green leaves with serrated edges. Grows in dense tangled clumps from green runners.
Reddish-bronze to salmon body with darker speckles. Elongated oval spot at tail base. Fins often pale yellow.
Deep notches on very curved snout. Tall rounded dorsal fin. Straight rear edge on pelvic fin (compare: S. mokarran).
The largest of the mantis shrimps. Golden antennal plates with dark speckles. Found in rounded burrows in the sand.
Light brown body with rectangular brown spots (paw prints) arranged in stripes. Dusky tail with pale margin.
Triangular carapace, similar to P. mutica (above), but covered in lumpy projections. Found among coral rubble.
Yellow legs and body with two broad red stripes down the back. Long white antennae. Often found in pairs.
Maximum depth from Ref. 108813. Open reefs, 2 to 15 m deep (Ref. 415). Found in mangroves (Ref. 86836). In Belize, it was found in a submarine cave near Columbus Cay (Ref. 87209).
Small clumps of undulating masses formed by fine, hair-like filaments. Whitish with rose-red to purple highlights.
Pale, with brown stripes and small white spots. Yellowish tail. Often found among Masked/Glass Gobies (p. 295).
Olive to yellowish body, pale near abdomen. May display body bars. Yellow fins and tail. Prominent canine teeth.
At night or while resting, the skin darkens and the blue lines become brighter, breaking up their general outline.
distinctive_features: Body shape: short and / or deep

description: Yellowish to tan body covered with bright blue lines and black spots. Long first dorsal spine. Distinctively long tail.
distinctive_features: Body shape: short and / or deep

description: Maximum depth from Ref. 116012. Zooxanthellate (Ref. 116012).
Rough tan to yellow body with dark blotches. Tufts of small bristles on flat antennal plates. Small black eyes.
Rounded bluish-silver body with yellow stripes. Dark spot below lateral line (compare: spot on L. rhomboides).
distinctive_features: Body shape: short and / or deep

description: Nearly transparent body with a long bulbous proboscis and dark eyes. Foot appears as a single swimming fin.
Encrusting tangles of thin white tubes that the tiny worms build as shelters. Tiny red radioles are visible at each tip.
Large rounded dark to olive-green bubbles, often with a silvery sheen. May grow individually or in small clusters.
Transparent smooth rounded body, generally taking on color of the host gorgonian. White eyes on short stalks.
Banded reddish-brown arms that only branch once or twice. Found on sea rods (p. 97). Curled up by day.
Smooth transparent bell with four long thin trailing tentacles. Sting causes intense pain. Found in shallows.

Found on surface of hard corals and sea fans on coral reefs, from shallow reef crests to drop offs at depths to 30 m. Exhibit greater habitat specificity than other chaenopsids. Spirobranchus giganteus. Females and immature males move about on surface of or near live corals, particularly sea fans. Breeding males are particularly found only in tubes abandoned by serpulid worms Spirobranchus giganteus. These probably controls their population size (Ref. 51514).
Reddish stalks branch repeatedly, growing in a single plane like a fan. Covered with tiny white stinging polyps.
Large head and short rhinophores. White patch on head and on parapodia. Only found on Turtle Grass (p. 11).
Highly variable in color: tan, reddish-brown to yellow. Pale blue lines below eye. Branching cirrus over eye.
Delicate fan-shaped white colonies growing from a common stalk. Found in lagoons and on shallow reefs.
Green to reddish-brown body with twelve feather-like feeding tentacles. Found in algae and seagrass beds.
White spots on head, white band behind eye. Many large spines. Cirrus with short branches. Orange patch over nape.
Translucent with dark bars, more widely spaced towards rear. Pink head with brown bands. Dark spots below eye.
Elongated silvery body with faint yellow body stripes. Two dorsal fins are widely separated. Usually schooling.
Five black body bars tapering towards white abdomen. Bright yellow patch on upper body. Widely forked tail.
distinctive_features: Body shape: short and / or deep

description: Dense bright green clumps of short stalks, with lateral branchlets forming serrated edges. Grows on runners.
Bushy structures with flat blades. Distinctively small marginal teeth. Yellow to brownish with light banding.
Fuzzy cylindrical branches in dense clusters. Found growing on hard substrates in shallow, sheltered areas.
Yellow snout and stripe over eye to behind pectoral. Blue stripe onto tail. Cleaner species. Absent W Caribbean.
Large fish without a host are gray. Small fish with white-edged black stripe and narrow white borders on tail.
Long sharply pointed white shell with reddish highlights. Dark eyespots. Parasitic on sea cucumbers (p. 152).
Light brown body with darker blotches, concentrated down the medial line. Widely spaced pseudotentacles.
Dark upper body, paler below. Blue lines on snout and radiating from eye. Black borders on a yellowish tail.
distinctive_features: Body shape: short and / or deep

description: Large white spots on brown to greenish body. Small yellow spots on head, circled in white. Protruding nostrils.
Silvery-gray body with five to seven distinct dark bars. Found on rocky shores in coastal areas (not islands).
distinctive_features: Body shape: short and / or deep

description: Silver with a bluish to lavender sheen. Dark spot on upper pectoral base. No blue or yellow markings on face.
distinctive_features: Body shape: short and / or deep

description: Minimum depth from Ref. 109199. It is found on coral reefs and rocky bottoms at depths of 3 to 6 m (Ref. 83916).
Dense clusters of shiny dark green filaments, growing to form a dense cushion. Found in calm shallow areas.
Bulbous head with no obvious rostrum. Dorsal fin has a distinctively long base. Body thickens towards tail base.
Bluish body. Slender conical snout. Dorsal fin starts just behind the pectoral. Both tail lobes are the same size.
A rare lavender variation of Shortfin Pipefish, often in shallower water. Darker varieties are found up to 300m.
Silver to gray hatchet-shaped body with no distinctive markings. Found schooling inside caves and caverns.
distinctive_features: Body shape: short and / or deep

description: Elongated pale eyestalks tapering to small reddish eyes. Banded antennae. Bands of short brown stripes on legs.
Short rounded rostrum. Last section (dactyl) of rear legs short, half previous section. Reef areas and gorgonians.
Two long horns on rostrum, connected at base. Oval carapace. Claw arms and the first pair of legs are long.
Dark brown to grayish body. Prominent rostrum (horn) above reddish lips. Pectoral fins may have darker edges.
distinctive_features: Body shape: short and / or deep

description: Pale body with sharply pointed snout. Brown spots on top of head. Brassy iris. Dorsal fin starts close to head.
Dark brown body with yellow head and fins. Black spot on snout, outlined in blue. Blue spots around eye and cheek.
distinctive_features: Body shape: short and / or deep

description: Slender body. Rounded dorsal fin starts well behind the pectoral fin. Second dorsal fin has a long trailing edge.
Rounded shell with a low spire. Tan to black with white blotches. Spiral lines of white dashes. Found in shallows.
Silvery body with a bluish tint above. Oblique dusky bars along upper back. Dusky outer edge on first dorsal fin.
Silvery body with large black spot at upper tail base, which may form a saddle. White spine on pelvic fin.

A schooling species usually occurring inshore. Feeds on zooplankton. Preyed upon by many larger fishes and squids (Ref. 5521). Important as live bait for scombrids (Ref. 26340). In 1937, at Kingston, Jamaica it spawns during the winter months. Travels long distance (Ref. 37032).
Maximum depth from Ref. 116012. Zooxanthellate (Ref. 116012). Throughout the Caribbean in a variety of reef habitats (Ref. 415), e.g., shallow protected reef environments (Ref. 848).
Disc-shaped body with a pentagonal design on the back. Six elongated holes around the edges. Very short spines.
Light red to brownish body. Thick tapering spines with light brown banding. Short white secondary spines.
Elongated body with narrow rounded plates. Plates are often mottled in white. Scaly gray to brown girdle.
Sharply elongated snout. White reticulated body pattern. Can change colors quickly to match surrounding corals.
distinctive_features: Body shape: short and / or deep

description: Highly variable in color and markings, solid brown or with white bands or blue spots, Sharply upturned rostrum.
The central discs of some individuals can be solid color, mottled or spotted pattern of red, green, white or brown.
Colors vary from pale to greenish. Two spots, black and pink, at upper corner of gill cover. Dark stripe to tail base.
Yellowish-brown with black spots. Spots on yellow-edged tail, dorsal and anal fins. Shy, often hides under ledges.
Chains of smaller rectangular green segments, joined down the middle by a single stalk. Forms dense bushes.
Silvery-gray with broad yellow stripes across head, continuing to tail base. All fins and tail are bright yellow.
Smooth reddish-brown disc. Arms are always in a contrasting color with darker bands and tiny spines.
Maximum depth from Ref. 116012. Zooxanthellate (Ref. 116012).
Delicate rounded shell with a low conical spire. Irregular reddish-brown bands. Light brown mantle.
Dark body with small pale spots. Dark patches at base of dorsal and pectoral fins. Pale honeycomb pattern on side.
distinctive_features: Body shape: short and / or deep

description: Common in reefs with depths between 6 to 24 m (Ref. 85482). Also found in mangroves (Ref. 86836).
Pale blue and gold stripes down side. Black oval at top of gill cover. Distinctively blunt head and upturned mouth.
Brown head and upper body. Large white patch on front of abdomen. Blue stripes on face. Spots on tail borders.
Circular crown of radioles. Brown with white bands or solid white. Found in clusters. Visible elongated tubes.
Elongated orange shell with pale stripes. Mantle and proboscis solid orange or pale with dark orange spots.
Rounded body with light brown overlapping plates. Attaches to the head of Blackbar Soldierfish (p. 325).
Single white or pinkish polyp surrounded by numerous thin curling tentacles. Often found with amphipods (p. 265).
Dark head with bulbous snout that extends out in front of the mouth. Golden iris. Found buried in shallow sand.
Only seen by the waste that this worm blows up onto the sand. A small pit nearby is the burrow’s entrance.
Gray body with white spots on head (may form radiating lines). Black stripes on tail (not continuing onto body).
Gray to olive-brown body with protruding eyes. Snout and tips of fins are pointed. Often found buried in sand.
Thin white tentacles radiating from a common hole in the reef. Body is hidden. Found close to sandy areas.
Thin black stripes on upper body. Wide stripe from eye. Nape and upper back yellow. Yellow saddle at tail base.
distinctive_features: Body shape: short and / or deep

description: Blue or purple upper body. Yellow abdomen, fins and tail. Acts as a cleaner species, removing larger parasites.
Rounded brown to orange body. Brown spots on legs. Smooth antennal plates. Distinctively purple antennae.
Flattened pale dorsum with purplish-red dots. Gills and rhinophores are darker, with small brownish speckles.
Uniformly pale tan, speckled with small brown spots on upper head and body. Found in shallows and seagrass.
A sea butterfly with distinctive white speckles and broad disc-shaped wings. Can produce a feeding web up to 2 m wide.
Oval body with greenish-brown plates. Ribbed triangles on plates. Scaled girdle with brown and cream banding.
Elongated gray body with a long pointed rostrum. Dark streak near eye. Fast playful swimmers, often jumping.
Rose-red tangle of bulbous leaves on long cylindrical stems. Mixed with other algae inside the reef crest.
Dark to light brown arms with numerous long thin spines. Wide pale central lines on arms extend onto the disc.
Pale rounded valves with fine ribs with small spines. Interior mantle and short tentacles are red to lavender.
Oblique white bar behind eye. Many short spines, often “crewcut.” Single stalk cirrus may have short branches.
Dark purple to black body with thin purple and orange lines around the margin. Orange-tipped pseudotentacles.
Thin white lines on head. Yellow border on pectoral fins. Yellow ventral, dorsal and anal fins. Endemic to Yucatan.
Actual minimum depth (0.4 m) from Ref. 87163. On mangrove roots and peat greater than 0.3 m deep (Ref. 415).
Maximum thickness: 0.2 cm (Ref. 415). Mostly deeper than 6 m, sometimes in shallow rubble (Ref. 415). Also occurs in mangrove ponds (Ref. 86789).
Encrusts rocks, shells, dead coral or other sponges, in shallow lagoons and coral reefs (Ref. 85482). Also found in mangroves (Ref. 86836).
Occurs in shallow inshore hard bottoms (Ref. 85482) and mangrove ponds (Ref. 86789). In Belize, it is found in a submarine cave near Columbus Cay (Ref. 87209).
Minimum depth from Ref. 116509. Growing on deeper areas of the reef over sandy and or muddy bottoms, covered by sediments (Ref. 415).
In Bocas del Torro, Panama the species is on seagrass beds; 1 to 3 m deep. The species reported from 50 to 100 deep (Ref. 415). Small and cryptic, partly buried in sediment (Ref. 086786).
Common on reefs; encrusts dead corals or the bases of live corals or on metamorphic rocks (Ref. 85482).
Minimum depth from Ref. 116511. Fore-reef sponge. Found on subvertical to horizontal hard substrates (Ref. 116510).
Commensal with amphipods (Ref. 82302). Known from depths between 20 to 40 m (Ref. 081714).
Silver to bronze body with a yellowish snout. Multiple speckles of different sizes are found on each scale.
Translucent body with thin red stripes. Two distinctively curved bands near abdomen. Commonly found in pairs.
Pale central disc with a brown circle inside. Short brown tentacles. Found encrusting vase and rope sponges.
Two adjacent horns on rostrum. Postero-lateral spine is sharply pointed. Usually found encrusted with sponges.
Black ocellus on gill cover. Dark bars extend onto dorsal fin. White stripe on snout. Males develop a bright red rear body.
Large black spots on body and all fins. Wide dark bars on head and body. Fewer spines than the Porcupinefish.
distinctive_features: Body shape: short and / or deep

description: White body with yellow fins. Single black bar through eye to dorsal fin. Small black spot on rear dorsal fin edge.
distinctive_features: Body shape: short and / or deep

description: Pale brown body. Two large dark spots on both dorsal and anal fins. Spot behind pectoral base and on tail.
distinctive_features: Body shape: short and / or deep

description: White with large light brown to orange spots down side. Large black spot at lower-rear edge of first dorsal fin.
Red body with diffuse white stripe from mouth onto side. Large yellow patch from end of dorsal fin onto upper tail.
Red body with white spots on abdomen and dark spots above. White patches on claws with no green-gold spots.
Rounded yellow spot on snout. Yellow/bluish/white stripe to dark spot at tail base. On sponges. N/W Caribbean.
Long arms with very short spines. The smooth central disc and the arms are covered in tiny brownish spots.
Transparent body with large spots down the back and sides. Purple spots on head. Bands on claws and legs.
Heavily mottled and spotted body. Flexible dorsal spines. Skin keel along lower body, with a row of dark spots.
Dark bars: across eye, from nape to pelvic fin and from tip of dorsal fin to tail base. Rear body and tail spotted.
Dark gray to black above with white spots and circles. White below. Flattened upturned snout. Small eyes.
White to pink body with three large reddish-black spots below dorsal fin. Blue lines from mouth to below eye.
Flattened oval shell with low blunt apex. Smooth, with low radial ribs. Olive-green to reddish with white spots.
Irregular black spots over a pale background. Patterns vary greatly. Dorsal fin often edged in black. White iris.
Pale pink to reddish carapace and claws, all covered in bright white and purple spots, each circled in red.
The largest of the scorpionfishes. Pale tail with three dark bars. Often fleshy cirrus over eye. Color highly variable.
Tan to greenish-brown body. Numerous dark circles and pale spots. Parapodial wings usually closed over back.
Brown with dark spots behind eye and only few spots on body. Fins match body color. Found in shallow rubble.
Dark red to brown. Upper legs and body are covered with small white spots. Brown stripes on the lower legs.

From about the size of a pea. Yellowish body with black or brown spots. The tail is tucked in against the body. Yellow or white body with dense black spots. Often dark bands running between eyes and across upper back.
distinctive_features: Body shape: short and / or deep

description: Transparent to milky-white translucent body. Most often with four brown to black spots on the rounded oral lobes.
Reddish body with large head and small tail. Pale spots with blue rims. Short antennae. Found on anemones.
Pink with reddish stripes. First dorsal fin yellow. Second dorsal, pelvic and anal fins white to pale pink.
Thick-bodied creamy white shell with rounded knobs. Spiral cords with white and brown spots on final whorl.

Zooxanthellate (Ref. 116012). Upper to mid reef slopes, lagoons with clear water (Ref. 846), subtidal reef tops and reef edges (Ref. 86439). In Belize, it was also found in a submarine cave at a depth of 10 m (Ref. 87209).
Found in deep water (Ref. 847) but can be found in shallow waters (Ref. 122680), particularly in infra- and circalittoral zones (Ref. 85338).

Maximum body thickness: 0.2 cm (Ref. 415). On dead coral in reef environments: 2 to 15 m deep (Ref. 415). Also found in mangroves (Ref. 86836). Favors coral undersides, overhangs, and crevices in shallow and deep reefs and hard bottoms (Ref. 85482).
Tall bright green oval leaves. Three to four close-set pairs growing on a long upright stalk. Grows on runners.
Radioles form a U-shaped crown. Red and yellow are the most common colors. Calcified tube usually hidden.
Red to purple with short brown bristles. Banded legs. Blue eyes with a distinctive star-shaped pattern.
Dark to light brown valves with numerous raised ridges. Sharply pointed end is hidden in reef, sand or sponges.
Common on mangroves (Ref. 415). Massive forms usually found on reefs; while branching and more lobate forms inhabit quieter shallow inshore hard bottoms, sediment and seagrass beds (Ref. 85482).
Pale brown body with small olive spots. Wide flattened head. Found among coral rubble in very shallow water.
Background color from white to pink or yellowish. Brown blotches become smaller near margin. Pointed tentacles.
Pointed shell with numerous small ridges. Usually white to pale yellow or brown, circled with dark brown spots.
Green body with pink stripes on cheek. Pink edge on gill cover, yellow spot at top corner. Yellow markings on tail.
Found in mangroves (Ref. 86836).

Reefs, mangrove peat banks, occasionally on roots (Ref. 415). Association: with two algae Ostreobiium constrictum Lucas a Chlorophyta, Acrochetum spongicolum Webervan Bosse, Rhodophyta) (Ref. 415), and an amphipod Leucothoe ubouhu living within the sponging fibers of M. laxissima was found among Belizean specimens (Ref. 82302).
Thin dark lines and spots on head, body and fins. Color varies (may be black). Lure with worm-like appendages.
distinctive_features: Body shape: short and / or deep

description: Tan body with thin brown lines and a large hump on the back. Long tapering tail. Tiny blue spots circled in red.
Faint chevron grooves along the plates. Color varies widely from orange to purple, pink or gray.
Yellow body with brown bands. Gold on legs and tail. Found on or near sea cucumbers, urchins and sea stars.
Translucent to reddish-bronze body with thin dark stripes. Dark spot at tail base, sometimes diffuse. Yellowish fins.
distinctive_features: Body shape: short and / or deep

description: Five dark yellow to bronze stripes along upper body. Pale abdomen. Blunt snout. Schools on deeper reefs.
Pale body with three dark stripes from snout to tail base. Reddish eyes. Clear tail (compare: S. taeniopterus).
Longitudinal red lines and white blotches. Branching red appendages on oral veil. Red on gills and rhinophores.
Dense tangles of fine hair-like filaments, repeatedly branching outwards. Uniform reddish-orange in color.
Translucent tan to pink body with white speckles. Large claw arms. Lives under encrusting tunicates (p. 135).
Maximum depth based on Ref. 108813. Found in mangroves (Ref. 86836) and reef slope (Ref. 87198).
Flattened oral disc is completely covered with short rounded green or brown tentacles. Prefers shallows.
Transparent body with brown, white and reddish spots. Patches on the back. Found on Sun Anemones (p. 117).
Large flattened disc, often mottled with patches of white. Lines radiate from a pale central mouth. Short tentacles.
Cream to yellowish valves with broad radiating pink lines. Only empty valves are found; usually buried under sand.
Yellow upper body and bright blue or purple abdomen. Bright blue line runs from snout and over top of eye.
Pale brown body with six hourglass saddles down back. Dark bars under eye. Dark patch on upper pectoral base.
Ten long pale arms with thin red banding. Red side branches. Can swim about by undulating the arms.
Zooxanthellate (Ref. 116012). In Belize, it was found in submarine cave near Columbus Cay at a depth of 10 m (Ref. 87209).
White to light green with papillae on head. Red flecks on parapodial edge. Dark band on short knobby rhinophores.
Brown body with yellowish abdomen. Black spots on snout, pectoral base and usually at tail base. Yellow iris.
distinctive_features: Body shape: short and / or deep

description: Silvery body with distinctively large scales and a large upturned mouth. Dark grayish fins. Mostly nocturnal.
distinctive_features: Huge silver scales; upturned mouth; often at night or wrecks

description: Orange to reddish body with small white tubercles and lines. Stocky rhinophores. Feeds on hydroids (p. 136).
Translucent gray with white speckles. Sharp tubercles on long inflated cerata. Caruncle between tall rhinophores.
Translucent body with small spines and large claws. Found in shallow coral debris and dead conch shells.
Zooxanthellate (Refs. 116012, 121684). Found in low light conditions (Ref. 1663). On rocky reef , shallow to mesophotic waters (Ref. 121684).
Rounded translucent bodies with reticulated pattern of branching white internal organs. Short dark tentacles.
Greenish-brown head and body, covered in blue-outlined bright red to orange spots. Large dark patch behind eye.
Thimble-shaped bell is transparent on top and brown beneath. Few short tentacles. Often in large blooms.
Found in shallow protected reef environments, especially seagrass beds (Ref. 83917).

Agaricia tenuifolia has been observed to a maximum depth of 15 m, based on personal communication with our expert, Charlie Veron. Zooxanthellate (Ref. 116012). Common along the Caribbean continental coast. Very common in Bocas del Torro, Panama; does not occur in Florida or the Bahamas (Ref. 415). This species exists almost exclusively in conspecific aggregations (Ref. 87225).
Long thin central stalks. Tiny alternating branchlets with tiny polyps. Usually found growing in small clusters.
Chains of thick calcified dark green segments with three distinct lobes. Forms low bushes from a common base.
Brown or tan to yellow body is creased and covered in small knobby podia, usually in sharply contrasting colors.
Yellow body with three short blue lines, from snout and behind eye. Found on deeper reefs, often under ledges.
Brown to tan. Dark spot at base of pectoral fin. Usually a dark spot at upper tail base. Yellow crescent above eye.
distinctive_features: Body shape: short and / or deep

description: Color varies from olive-green to reddish. Red or purple markings on legs and claws. Found on rocky inshores.
Translucent head and body with prominent thin black bars. Found in coral rubble or sponges. From Florida.
Bright yellow body with an irregular dark brown stripe running from lower lip, through eye to near tail base.
Heavy body with short wide snout. Series of dark body bars and markings. Upper lobe of tail is the largest.
The largest sea cucumber. Pale brown body with dark bands. Short tentacles around mouth. Mostly nocturnal.
Yellow with black lines between pale tubercles. Honduras only, similar D. nigrolineata has white-ringed tubercles.
White body with broad orange stripe from chin to tail base. Dark saddles on upper body. Dark stripes on tail.
Silvery with bronze-yellow stripe from snout to tail base, another along upper side. Large black spot at tail base.
Golden brown scales with paler edges. Distinctively long pointed teeth on the lateral edges of each scale.
Grayish-brown to blackish body. Scale rows form dark lines. Large triangular anal fin. Tall rounded dorsal fin.

Maximum depth reported taken from Ref. 128739. On reef environments, usually under 5 m deep (Ref. 415). Also found in mangroves (Ref. 86836). Dwells in coral reefs or rock pavements in shallow depths. Possibly associated with polychaete worms (Ref. 128739).
Colonies of small white bristled worms. Only found on the inside walls of Touch-Me-Not Sponges (p. 67).
Fleshy mantle covers the entire shell. May be confused for a sea slug; distinctive single raised siphon at front.
Elongated shell with white triangles on edges of black or mottled mantle. (Previously Cyphoma “black morph.”)
Pale blue legs, banded with red and yellow at the joints. Large black claws with white spots. Blue eyestalks.
Pointed shell with faint spiral lines and long aperture lip. Pink mantle with white speckles. Short pointed tentacles.
Elongated body with pale stripes and scattered spots. Eyes set far back from elongated snout. Color is highly variable.
Rounded semi-transparent tubes of pale pink to red, branching near the tips and growing into tangled clumps.
Clusters of calcified cream to rose-red tubes, branching to form rounded domes. Gametophyte stage shown above.
Highly branched cerata surround the dorsum. Flattened oral veil with short tentacles. Sheathed rhinophores.
Tangled mass of filamentous threads, pinkish inside and brown outside. Often grows on and around coral heads.
Silvery blue body with golden saddles. Dark bars below eye. Black upper pectorals, usually held out to the sides.
Flat thin green leaves about 1.5 cm wide, with rounded tips. Covers vast areas, dominating other seagrasses.
Translucent green body with lighter patches, most often with longitudinal lines. Found flattened on Turtle Grass.
Brownish-gray with dense dark speckles. Often white bar at midbody. Low jaw on blunt snout. Found on sponges.
Dark golden-yellow shell with nine axial ribs. Chocolate- brown between the ribs. Red mantle with white speckles.
Pale brown with dark brown spots and bars. Two dark ocelli at tail base. Large dark blotch on front of dorsal fin.
Translucent reddish-brown body. Banded claws on long claw arms. Nocturnal, often found inside tube sponges.
Last two body bars darker. Red to dusky bar at tail base. Breeding males with a bright yellow head. S Caribbean.
Five elongated arms bordered with a series of knobby plates, each with two sharp white spines. Color variable.
Azooxanthellate. Found in slope (Ref. 116012).
Dark spots under second dorsal fin, at base of tail and on edge of gill cover. White lines on top and bottom of eye.
Wide flattened oral disc with short rectangular tentacles arranged around the outer edges. Typically solitary.
Small mouth on elongated snout, distinctively concave below and convex above. Pale dorsal and anal fins.
distinctive_features: Body shape: short and / or deep

description: Spiny rostrum and a distinctive constriction in the area behind eyes. Translucent with pink and purple highlights.
Flattened bell with short clustered tentacles, pale brown with blunt white tips. Found upside down in the shallows.
Dark sides with broad white stripes. Thin white stripes on top of back. Found on West Indian Sea Eggs (p. 151).

Depth based on the Caribbean from Ref. 415. Restricted to the lower buttress zone, fore reef, and fore reef slope of fringing barrier-type reefs at depths between 1.5 to 70 m (Ref. 892). Also found in mangroves (Ref. 86836).
Circular crown of radioles, sometimes banded. Tiny eyespots usually visible. Tubes are sometimes hidden.
White to pale brown scales with thin black margins. Dark crescents on inner half of each overlapping scale.
Pale body with numerous white podia. Short red, purple or white spines in sparse rows, following the plate lines.

Maximum depth reported taken from Ref. 128739. Rare on mangroves (Ref. 415), but common on shallow hard grounds, seagrass beds, and inshore reefs (Ref. 85482). Inhabits seagrass meadows, coralline algae reefs and lower mesophotic reefs (Ref. 128739).
Green to yellowish body. White lines from round head to rhinophores. Small gap in closed parapodia at midbody.
Translucent body with pale red markings and long thin antennae. Buries quickly into sand when disturbed.
Intertidal (Ref. 109199).
Transparent ribbon-like body with a thin central band. Lower edge of cilia may show bluish-green fluorescence.
Brown body with wide dark bands. Variable patterns, claws may have green speckling and pale orange tips.
Uniformly greenish-brown body. May have darker band across nape. Only tips of jaws are touching. Pale iris.
Rounded shell with bulbous spire. Wavy zigzag lines and spots. Dominant color may be black, green or reddish.
Color varies, often brown with pale spots. While moving, a sucker on rear foot anchors it while the body stretches.
Tapering white body with four dark or red saddles across back. Tall flexible first dorsal fin. Often buried in sand.
Greenish-brown body with white patches on dorsum. Row of pink circles and yellow spots. Thick warty cerata.
The largest comb jelly. Long warty protrusions on body and large oral lobes, which may have brown markings.
Flattened disc with tentacles ending in forked projections. Shorter tentacles around rim. May be found in clusters.
Rounded bell with dark yellow or purple spots. Color ranges from translucent pink to yellow. Four oral arms.
Green to brown body, wider towards the rear. Scattered rough papillae. Short rolled rhinophores and tentacles.
Rounded tubercles cover the dorsum. Highly variable in color: yellow, gold or purple. Short fused rhinophores.
Dense clumps of rounded three-lobed leaves. Dark to yellowish-green. Found in darker areas of the reef.
Rounded glossy white valves with thin faint ribs. Found in rubble. Long foot used to move quickly into shelter.
Glossy oval shell with a blunt spire. Cream to tan base color with a series of dark brown wavy longitudinal lines.
Yellowish body with honeycomb pattern of dark lines. Large dark patches on back and sides. Fleshy spines held erect.
distinctive_features: Body shape: short and / or deep

description: White speckles on a tapering gray to pinkish dorsum. Small branching cerata and a broad rounded oral veil.
Robust shell with a long tapering aperture lip. Mantle is mottled black and orange and is commonly buried.
Pale orange shell with white highlights. Long thick-walled lip and curved aperture. Pale tan mantle and proboscis.
Orange to brownish shell with evenly sized pointed spines on the whorls. Aperture lip slants upwards.
Thickened shell with many short irregular protruding knobs. Thickened shell lip. Often covered in red algae.
Rounded dark purple to black body. White spines are short and dense. Visible podia. Prefers shallow reefs.
Robust conical shell with knobs around the lower edge. White mantle with speckles. Often covered in red algae.
Dark upper body and short snout. Gray dorsal and anal fins. Pectoral fin brownish with pale tip and lower edge.
Pale tan to whitish shell with brown reticulated markings. White spots on the mantle, often completely retracted.
Undulating gelatinous mass of soft tubular dull brown stalks covered with fine filaments. Can cover large areas.
Dark gray to black body covered in white spots and thin white lines. Three distinct ridges running along sides.
Dense mat of long filamentous coppery-brown threads. Attaches to the substrate or hard corals and gorgonians.
Uniformly silver body with no distinctive markings. Long sloping forehead. Small white iris. Often in sandy areas.
Round white cup shape on a long white stalk. Rounded ridges with pale valleys radiating from the center.
Rounded spiraling thick-walled blades found growing in dense clumps. Blades concentrically banded in white.
Usually orange with white specks, in a tunic with shared excurrent siphons. Found growing in small clusters.
Rigid stalk with only a few branches. Distinctively bright white polyps run in pairs down both stalk and branches.
Rigid colonies of thin cylindrical branches. Rectangular zooids are clearly visible. Prefers deep reefs and walls.

Shallow water octocoral (Ref. 808), usually in turbid coastal areas (Ref. 1010998). On bay and nearshore (Ref. 109199). At depths of 1.5 to 8 m on coral reefs and rocky bottoms (Ref. 83916). Occurs on shaded clear waters where currents are strong (Ref. 101098). Common in the fouling community of harbors, on piers or wrecks (Ref. 415, 101098) which are not exposed to sunlight (Ref. 415)
Pale oblong shell with three broad brown bands (may be diffuse). Orange spots on white mantle and proboscis.
Dark, almost black mantle covers most of the plates’ surface, leaving a thin white line visible down the center.
Yellow body with tufts of thick dense cerata with white speckles and tips. Yellow rhinophores and oral tentacles.
Claws of equal size. Carapace, legs and eyestalks are covered in tiny white spots. Short light brown bristles.
Glossy elongated brown shell with a long spire. Round to oblong pale spots. Brown proboscis with white blotches.
Glossy cream to brownish shell with white spots and faint banding. Translucent mantle with large white spots.
Robust brown to reddish body. Numerous irregular white spots and small bumps cover the body and arms.
Robust cream to brown body with white blotches. Dark lines along parapodia and rhinophore and tentacle tips.
Dark brown with white blotches and small orange spots. White lines run from rhinophores to large pale gills.
Elongated oval leaves with a whitish central vein. Olive to light brown. Often found growing in small clusters.
The most common sharksucker on Green Sea Turtles. May be seen aggressively feeding on the turtle’s waste.
Black midbody stripe with pale borders. White borders on tail are wide (compare to narrow on E. naucrates).
Translucent reddish body with distinctively white-tipped claws. Found only inside Touch-Me-Not Sponges (p. 67).
Black body with solid white lines on head and radiating from eyes. White spots on body. Found only in Belize.
Distinctively white snout. Brown body with regularly spaced white and dark bars. Found on sandy bottoms.
Orange body, often with white spots. Pairs of hooked orange spines at tail base, larger in males. Black tail.
distinctive_features: Body shape: short and / or deep

description: Black body and pointed barbels, covered with small white spots. Found only in Belize and surrounding atolls.
Two small spots behind second dorsal fin, first black then smaller white. Darker tips on second dorsal and anal fins.
Tapering body. Long thin claws and a flattened tail. May have white bands. Usually found on Wire Coral (p. 91).
Sparse elongated brown leaves, often forking near the base. Attaches to the reef with a strong conical holdfast.
Creamy-brown dorsum, often with darker patches. Large orange tubercles in a cauliflower shape, ringed in white.
Translucent with black lateral stripe from snout, through eye to tail. Mimics small juveniles of Bluehead Wrasse.
Flattened carapace with wavy ridges. Brown bands on legs. Commensal with Inflated Sea Biscuits (p. 151).
Flat interwoven mat of stubby notched blades. Light brown to green, often with a brilliant blue iridescence.
Widely branching segments are slightly flattened. Pale pink to white in color. Found growing in dense clumps.
Small twin-lobed yellow blades with thin olive-brown bands. Often found with n sheen of green or bluish iridescence.
Light yellow to tan body, often with dark blotches. Short rhinophores. Distinctive white rim along parapodial edge.
Circular yellow crown of dense radioles with numerous thin dark bands. The tube is usually hidden in the reef.
Minimum depth from Ref. 108813. Occurs in shallow hard grounds, coral reefs (Ref. 85482), and in mangroves (Ref. 86836).
Distinctive yellow stripe from eye to tail base. Deeply forked yellow tail. Two thin fleshy barbels under chin.
Silvery body with a yellowish tint. All fins and tail are pale yellow. Often yellow marks around mouth and eye.
Gray to light blue body with diffuse pale yellow lines on dorsal and anal fins. Yellow tail. Blue markings on head.
Short bushy colonies of flattened branches. Bright yellow with translucent white polyps on all branch edges.
Transparent body with visible internal organs. One oversized major claw with yellow or purple highlights.
Bright yellow tentacles around a darker yellow oral disc. The body is yellow. Found on deeper sponge species.
Variable yellow to brown body with dark blotches and small yellow spots. Snout and tips of fins are rounded.
Upper head, back and front dorsal fin bluish-brown. Rest of body and fins yellow. Blue lines and spots on head.
distinctive_features: Body shape: short and / or deep

description: Bluish-gray body with rows of yellow spots. Wavy yellow lines across cheek and onto gill cover. Bright yellow iris.
Two ocellated blue spots, one at rear base of dorsal fin, one at tail base. Yellow patch from snout tip onto dorsal.
Tan, becoming yellowish in grass, with midbody stripe and bars. Small black spot on front of dorsal fin. Pink iris.
Pale lines outline irregular dark blotches on upper body, wavy lines on flank. Broad yellow margin on pectoral fin.
The largest of the mojarras. Silvery body with diffuse broken dark bars. Distinctive yellow pelvic and anal fins.
Slender elongated body with dark spots and small bright yellow flap-like cirri. Found on Great Star Coral (p. 82).
Yellow head and pale bluish body. Dark marks on head. Bar through eye. Rounded tail. Found in sandy burrows.
Yellow body. Brilliant blue stripe, outlined in black, runs from behind eye to tail base. All fins are translucent.
Long tapering rostrum. Carapace with yellow and black stripes. Long legs with yellow stripes. Small blue claws.
Yellow to white stripe from top of eye to tail. Yellowish snout. Sometimes small spot on snout. On sponges.
Sharply defined dark brown upper body and white lower body. White stripe from chin to bright yellow dorsal fin.
Yellow bar on yellowish snout. Yellow stripe from top of eye to end of tail. Cleaner species. SE Caribbean.
Dark blue with iridescent blue spots over entire body and head. Bright blue borders on fins. Translucent tail.
distinctive_features: Body shape: short and / or deep

description: Uniformly greenish body. Dorsal, anal and pelvic fins tinged with red. Black spot at base of pectoral. Red eye.
Blue V on snout. Tail, rear dorsal, anal and pelvic fins yellow. White abdomen and tail base. Florida/N Mexico.
Bright yellow line from snout, through eye to a widely forked yellow tail. Large pale yellow spots on upper body.
Small elongated bell with a pointed tip. Four long trailing tentacles with distinctive rows of small white bulbs.
Mottled orange-brown parapodia, often opened. Olive head and body. Purple acorn-shaped patch on the head.
Found in lower reef slopes protected from wave action (Ref. 847).
Olive-green, often with iridescent blue spots. Sometimes has a black tail. Large eyes. Purplish-brown rhinophores.
